issue_title: Wrapper for unichr
issue_body:
To make it easier to understand, a custom wrapper for `unichr` could be created. It could be named `CustomChar`.

So this code

``` python
>>> lcd.create_char(0, smiley)
>>> lcd.write_string(unichr(0))
```

becomes

``` python
>>> lcd.create_char(0, smiley)
>>> lcd.write_string(CustomChar(0))
```

We could even return it from the `create_char` function:

``` python
>>> smiley_char = lcd.create_char(0, smiley)
>>> lcd.write_string(smiley_char)
```

It would behave like a unicode object, so it could be concatenated:

``` python
>>>> lcd.write_string(u'Hello ' + smiley_char)
```

issue_title: `parse_cluster_nodes` may fail for redis 4.0 and above
issue_body:
I just opened [this ticket](https://github.com/antirez/redis/issues/5293) on `redis`, which describes a doc error in Redis for 4.0.0 and greater -- the `CLUSTER NODES` command output should actually be documented as containing `<ip:port@cport>` rather than `<ip:port>`. The new cluster port field seems to be a mandatory field as of Redis 4.0.0.

This causes [this line](https://github.com/NoneGG/aredis/blob/master/aredis/commands/cluster.py#L60) to set `port="6379@16379"` in the standard case, which then throws a ValueError [here](https://github.com/NoneGG/aredis/blob/master/aredis/commands/cluster.py#L65).

issue_title: [jsonxsl] yang union type isn't fully represented depending on order on types
issue_body:
## Issue
When using jsonxsl plugin to create stylesheet to convert XML to JSON, the YANG union type sometimes doesn't get fully represented.

For example, using following model
```
module a {
  prefix "a";
  namespace "urn:a";
  revision "2018-04-26";

  container x {
    leaf y {
      type union {
        type enumeration {
          enum TEST;
        }
        type uint32;
      }
    }
  }
}
```

The generated stylesheet looks like
```
$ pyang -f jsonxsl a.yang | xmllint --format - | tee a.xsl                                                                                                                                                                                                                             [ruby-2.5.0p0]
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<stylesheet xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:a="urn:a" xmlns:en="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:notification:1.0" xmlns:nc="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" version="1.0">
  <output method="text"/>
  <include href="/usr/local/share/yang/xslt/jsonxsl-templates.xsl"/>
  <strip-space elements="*"/>
  <template name="nsuri-to-module">
    <param name="uri"/>
    <choose>
      <when test="$uri='urn:a'">
        <text>a</text>
      </when>
    </choose>
  </template>
  <template match="//nc:*/a:x">
    <call-template name="container">
      <with-param name="level">1</with-param>
      <with-param name="nsid">a:</with-param>
    </call-template>
  </template>
  <template match="//nc:*/a:x/a:y">
    <call-template name="leaf">
      <with-param name="level">2</with-param>
      <with-param name="type">string</with-param>
    </call-template>
  </template>
</stylesheet>
```

In the above, only `string` JSON type is represented. So, when using an XML document like:
```
<data xmlns="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0">
  <x xmlns="urn:a">
    <y>100</y>
  </x>
</data>
```

The conversion is:
```
$ xsltproc a.xsl a.xml                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 [ruby-2.5.0p0]
{
  "a:x": {
    "y": "100"
  }
}
```

Which doesn't match YANG model since `uint32` should be a JSON number.

## Behavior on changing order
Changing the order of enum so that `uint32` occurs first results in:
```
$ pyang -f jsonxsl a.yang | xmllint --format - | tee a.xsl                                                                                                                                                                                                                             [ruby-2.5.0p0]
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<stylesheet xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform" xmlns:a="urn:a" xmlns:en="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:notification:1.0" xmlns:nc="urn:ietf:params:xml:ns:netconf:base:1.0" version="1.0">
  <output method="text"/>
  <include href="/usr/local/share/yang/xslt/jsonxsl-templates.xsl"/>
  <strip-space elements="*"/>
  <template name="nsuri-to-module">
    <param name="uri"/>
    <choose>
      <when test="$uri='urn:a'">
        <text>a</text>
      </when>
    </choose>
  </template>
  <template match="//nc:*/a:x">
    <call-template name="container">
      <with-param name="level">1</with-param>
      <with-param name="nsid">a:</with-param>
    </call-template>
  </template>
  <template match="//nc:*/a:x/a:y">
    <call-template name="leaf">
      <with-param name="level">2</with-param>
      <with-param name="type">union</with-param>
      <with-param name="options">integer,other,</with-param>
    </call-template>
  </template>
</stylesheet>
```

## Code area
It looks like `union` types may automatically be mapped to `string` based on first type due to:
https://github.com/mbj4668/pyang/blob/master/pyang/plugins/jsonxsl.py#L183

issue_title: Downloading Attachments fails for a small number of datasets
issue_body:
This is only tested on nyc open data portal (https://nycopendata.socrata.com), but there are a handful of datasets for which the the attachment metadata either has no `assetId` property (see: `3av7-txd8`) or has an empty `assetId` property (see: `7isb-wh4c`). 

In either case, the path to the asset comes in the form `{base url}/api/assets/{blobId}?download=true` instead of `{base url}/api/views/{identifier}/files/{assetId}?download=true&filename={filename}`

If it has no `assetId property`, there is a Key Error and the client fails. If the `assetId` property is present, but empty, it appears that the `blobId` contains the asset ID, but since it is formatting the URL to a nonexistent resource, it is downloading an empty file.

issue_title: upgrade v0.3.6 to v0.3.7 lead to css selecter fail in some cases
Some changes to the resultdb
issue_body:
We are planning to do some changes and to send a pull request afterwards but we would like to hear your opinion first.

1. We would like to let the user specify the primary key used for saving to the resultdb because `taskid` might not be a good choice (we are having some duplicates because the same item comes from different tasks). The idea is to subclass `BaseHandler` and add a method which computes a primary key for every item using the fields specified by the spider (I guess we would need to override `on_result`). An user would use it like this:

  ```
class MySpider(BaseHandlerWithPrimaryKey):
        PRIMARY_KEY = [ 'name', 'age' ]
  ```

 The `ResultWorker` will then use the primary key which was calculated (if present) instead of the `taskid` to save the item to the resultdb.
2. We would like to add two more fields to the resultdb; `created` will store the datetime when a result was first scraped (this is one of the reasons we need a custom primary key) and `modified` will store the datetime when a result was last updated (i.e. one of its fields changed value or there is a new field). Since we are adding these two fields it would make sense to add another column which stores the `lastcrawltime` of the corresponding task so that all the information are stored in the same table. We would also change the result page so that it exposes this new information.

What do you think? Hints/suggestions?

issue_title: Fix the roll-off calculation
issue_body: We're currently getting a bit of aliasing at the high end of the frequency range when down-sampling.  (Up-sampling appears to be clean.)  This is most likely due to either a bad specification of the roll-off frequency, or something slightly off in the sinc window computation.

issue_title: Reading and writing exif values does not perserve values
issue_body:
When using the library to read exif data from an image (using load on a jpg), and rewriting the loaded exif data back to the image (using insert), not all exif values remain unchanged (as they should be).

For example, this example code lead (among others) to exif image rotation being lost and changed the denominator of the exposure time field from 1552 to 1075.

    exif = piexif.load("sample.jpg")
    piexif.insert(piexif.dump(exif), "sample.jpg")

should definitely be fixed.

issue_title: UKF fails when measurement space != state space
issue_body:
Consider the following: We want to track in 3D using 2D screen coordinates using computer vision. For a constant velocity model x = [x x' y y' z z'] and H@x = [pixel_x pixel_y]

But once we are in measurement space n = 2, not six, and we have too many samples 13(2n+1), when we should have 5.

I don't know if this is a fundamental problem with the UKF formulation, or if I haven't accounted for this problem in my code.

issue_title: Remove insecure code

Instead, use https://docs.python.org/2/library/ast.html#ast.literal_eval

issue_body:
**python**: 3.5.1
**Django**: 1.9
**pytest**: 2.9.2
**pytest-django**: 2.9.1
**freezegun**: 0.3.8

`models.py`
```python
class NetworkCredential(models.Model):
    """
    Store the zabbix username, zabbix password
    """
    customer = models.ForeignKey(Customer, on_delete=models.CASCADE, null=False)
    user = models.CharField(max_length=255, null=False, blank=False)
    password = models.CharField(max_length=255, null=False, blank=False)
    enable = models.BooleanField(default=True)
    refresh_time = models.IntegerField(validators=[MinValueValidator(1), MaxValueValidator(20)], default=3)

    created_datetime = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    updated_datetime = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    expiry_datetime = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True, default=None)

    class Meta:
        unique_together = (
            ("customer",
             "user",
             "password",
             "enable",
             "refresh_time"),
        )
```

`conftest.py`:
```python
@pytest.fixture
def prepare_network_credential(company):
    kwargs = {
        "customer": company,
        "user": "sivabudh.u",
        "password": 'codium#@!',
        "enable": True,
        "refresh_time": 2,
    }
    with freeze_time("2016-12-7 19:32:00"):
        return NetworkCredential.objects.create(**kwargs)
```

`tests.py`
```python
def test_created_datetime(prepare_network_credential):
    """Test created_datetime"""
    prepare_network_credential.save()
    nc = NetworkCredential.objects.first()

def test_updated_datetime(prepare_network_credential):
    """Test updated_datetime"""
    prepare_network_credential.save()
    nc = NetworkCredential.objects.filter(enable=True)
    with freeze_time("2016-12-8 20:20:00"):
        nc.update(user="Sarit")
    assert nc[0].user == "Sarit"
    import pdb; pdb.set_trace()
    assert nc[0].updated_datetime.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S') == '2016-12-08 20:20:00'
```

issue_title: Not able to parse javascript's ISO string format
issue_body:
Using JavaScript's `toISOString()` does not parse correctly. [The method returns a string in simplified extended ISO 8601 format.](https://developer.mozilla.org/en/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Date/toISOString).

Example of the code,

    >>> import parsedatetime.parsedatetime as pdt
    >>> cal = pdt.Calendar()
    >>> cal.parse('2016-05-07T07:52:00.000Z')
    ((2016, 5, 6, 20, 16, 0, 4, 127, 0), 2)

issue_title: Integration of pyquery for easier usage in tests (with client's responses) ?
Try using unroll+clblas GEMM
issue_body:
Following this article, http://petewarden.com/2015/04/20/why-gemm-is-at-the-heart-of-deep-learning/ http://www.reddit.com/r/MachineLearning/comments/338lfs/why_gemm_is_at_the_heart_of_deep_learning/ , decided should try this, in case gives an easy way to speed up DeepCL for large image sizes.

My verdict? Not useful :-(

Tried on my laptop, and on a K520, and the results were:
- unroll + matmult on cpu is a bit faster than direct cpu convolution.  I suppose this is because memory access patterns a better
- unroll + clblas was faster again
- the most naive convolutional opencl kernel, ie not using any type of unroll or gemm, was the fastest

For batchsize=128, inputplanes=32, inputsize=128, numfilters=32, filtersize=5, on a K520 got:
- convolution + cpu: 318s
- unrolled + cpu: 218s
- unrolled +clblas: invalid command queue
- no unrolling, propagate1: 2s

Matrices are apparently a bit too big for unroll + clblas, so tried using a smaller batchsize:
batchsize=16, inputplanes=32, inputsize=128, numfilters=32, filtersize=5:
- convolution + cpu: 39s
- unroll + cpu: 26s
- unroll + clblas GEMM: 2.2s
- propagate1: 0.27s

Note that propagate1 is DeepCL's most generic, least optimized kernel.  It doesnt use local memory (which is why it's generic, and works on anything really, unless it runs out of gpu global memory).  Kernels using local memory are around 3-10 times faster than propagate1.

Overall: current conclusion: unroll + clblas GEMM doesnt seem promising?

=> closing issue.

issue_title: mtools 1.2.2: `pip install mtools --upgrade` does not upgrade PyMongo
issue_body:
The 1.2.2 pip package for mtools apparently doesn't enforce the same requirements as [requirements.txt](https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/blob/develop/requirements.txt), so may have unexpected errors if an incompatible version of a dependency is installed.

To repro:
 - mtools 1.2.1 and pymongo 2.7.2 installed
 - `pip install mtools --upgrade`
 - run `mlaunch --replicaset --arbiter --port 30000 --nodes 2`

Commands like `mlaunch list` and `mlaunch stop` will fail with a traceback similar to:

```
mlaunch list
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/mlaunch", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('mtools==1.2.2', 'console_scripts', 'mlaunch')()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/mtools/mlaunch/mlaunch.py", line 1410, in main
    tool.run()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/mtools/mlaunch/mlaunch.py", line 259, in run
    getattr(self, self.args['command'])()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/mtools/mlaunch/mlaunch.py", line 555, in list
    self.discover()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/mtools/mlaunch/mlaunch.py", line 803, in discover
    self.cluster_tags['primary'].append( mrsc.primary[1] )
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/pymongo/database.py", line 211, in __getitem__
    return self.__getattr__(name)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/pymongo/database.py", line 201, in __getattr__
    return Collection(self, name)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/pymongo/collection.py", line 101, in __init__
    "of %s" % (basestring.__name__,))
```

Manually installing latest PyMongo should fix:
  `pip install pymongo --upgrade`

Check version of PyMongo:
  `python -c "import pymongo; print(pymongo.version);"`

issue_title: df support
issue_body:
Here are the things we need to make sure df support includes:

- [x] splitting out the output column
- [x] convert a list of dictionaries to a DataFrame (ideally with error logging to the user)
- [x] convert numbers stored as strings to proper numbers
- [x] convert numbers stored as strings with commas in them to proper numbers. 
- [x] dropping all rows where the output column is an ineligible value (nan, None, etc.). this particularly comes into play for our subpredictors. 
- [x] ignoring (dropping) any columns that should be ignored
- [x] date feature engineering
- [x] robust feature scaling
- [x] optional (gscv) feature truncating
- [x] removing all nan, None, and other values
- [ ] FUTURE: imputing missing values
- [x] getting dummies from categorical columns
- [x] convert to a scipy sparse matrix
- [x] support NLP feature transformations. For now we'll just do it as-is, but in the future, we'll probably split this out into it's own thing that we'l do separately, and just hstack the sparse results from tfidfvectorizer onto the sparse results from vectorizing our dataframe.

issue_title: Additional features for key/time signatures
issue_body:
cc @rafaelvalle 

I'm writing unit tests for the whole library, and found a few things I think should be changed/fixed with the time/key signature changes.

1. They currently aren't being written out in `PrettyMIDI.write`; they should be included here.  I started to do this myself, but I was unsure how to convert from our `KeySignature`/`TimeSignature` to python-midi data/events.

1. Related - I think it would be useful, for writing out any maybe in general, if `midi_key_to_key_number` got moved to a separate function in `utilities.py` and called something like  `mode_accidentals_to_key_number`; instead of taking in a `midi.event.KeySignature` it would just take in `num_accidentals` and `mode`.  Then, we should also have a `key_number_to_mode_accidentals`, which will be handy for writing out, I think.

1. `midi_key_to_key_number` and `key_name_to_key_number` don'tt have a Returns section in their docstring, I overlooked this when merging.

1. When constructing a PrettyMIDI object without a MIDI file, `key_changes` and `time_signature_changes` aren't getting created because they're created in the `_load_metadata` function which isn't called in that case.  So, we need to create empty lists for them manually, as is done for instruments; https://github.com/craffel/pretty-midi/blob/master/pretty_midi/pretty_midi.py#L96

I'll update if I find anything else!

issue_title: last sha for github PRs invalid if a new commit comes in during a run
issue_body:
```
        self.pull_request = self.github.pull_request(owner, repo, pr)
        self.commits = self.pr_commits(self.pull_request)
        self.last_sha = self.commits[-1].sha
        self.first_sha = self.commits[0].sha
        self.parent_sha = git.parent_sha(self.first_sha)
        self.diff = git.diff(self.parent_sha, self.last_sha)
```
if a new commit comes in, this will actually get the wrong SHA. we should ask the environment what the current sha is instead of asking the github PR API.

issue_title: Dynamic pin code and setup IDs.
issue_body:
Reading through the Apple HAP documentation, it's pretty clear that "if a device _can_ dynamically generate pin codes, it _should_."

To that end, I started playing around with stuff, and a dynamically generated pin code has no drawbacks (other than having to see what value was generated this time around).

I believe the same is true of setup id (which is used for the URL generation in https://github.com/ikalchev/HAP-python/pull/31 and https://github.com/ikalchev/HAP-python/pull/30). To that end, I believe that these values should _not_ be stored in the pickle.

The other thing I discovered while developing accessories is that pickle is not a great way of storing stuff, especially during development. It means that if you change a class, or an import, then things can stop working, and you'd need to delete the pickle file and then re-add the accessory. There are also possible security implications of pickling data, but I'm less concerned about that because we aren't unpickling user supplied data.

issue_title: then() doesn't take on promise returned by called handler
issue_body:
In the docs its stated that `The call to .then also returns a promise. If the handler that is called returns a promise, the promise returned by .then takes on the state of that returned promise.`  
So .then(my_func()) should take on the promise returned by my_func()  

Take a look at this example:
```
def testfunc():
    return Promise(lambda resolve, reject: resolve("second"))

Promise(lambda resolve, reject: resolve("first")).then(testfunc()).then(lambda res: print(res))
```

The first `.then` should take on the state of the promise returned by testfunc(), so in theory the second `.then` should print out "second". But this is not what happens, instead it prints out "first" from the first promise.

issue_title: Link to newer Docker image in the readme
issue_body:
I created my own Docker image ([`lukechilds/electrumx`](https://github.com/lukechilds/docker-electrumx)) due to some issues I had with the current image recommended in the readme ([`followtheart/electrumx`](https://github.com/followtheart/electrumx-docker)).

The followtheart image doesn't follow Docker best practices and is also quite large and out of date. I believe my image is better because:

**Size**

`lukechilds/electrumx`: 40MB
`followtheart/electrumx`: 175MB

**Follows Docker conventions**

- All settings are passed in as ENV vars (no need for ENV folder)
- Runs ElectrumX directly as PID 1 instead of bash -> daemon
  - Interactive Docker sessions attach directly meaning `Control-C` works
  - Interactive Docker sessions get ElectrumX output to STDOUT
  - ElectrumX output will be piped to normal Docker logging tools
  - You shouldn't dockerize a daemon, the Docker image itself should be daemonized
- Reproducible builds: separate branch for each release (followtheart container builds current master)


**Up to date**

Pulling from Docker Hub will pull the latest ElectrumX release. You can also specify a tag to pull specific older ElectrumX releases.

The latest followtheart build on Docker Hub is 6 months out of date.

**Secure**

If no SSL cert is provided one will be automatically generated on first boot.

---

Following these rules makes the image much easier to orchestrate in other Docker projects (e.g [Dockerized Vertcoin Electrum Server](https://github.com/lukechilds/docker-electrum-vertcoin/blob/master/docker-compose.yml)), it's also currently the most used ElectrumX image on Docker Hub.

Let me know if you'd like to link to this image instead and I'll happily submit a PR.

issue_title: metaclass conflict when decorating a base class
issue_body:
Thank you, this is a fantastic package. The blogs are just great.

The following code generates a metaclass conflict error in python2.7. It appears that something is confusing the metaclass resolution in the decorator. Do you have any suggestions about how this can be fixed? Other than the removing the decorator. I am afraid that that option is not available to me.

### Code
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python2.7
import abc
import wrapt

@wrapt.decorator
def pass_through(wrapped, instance, args, kwargs):
    return wrapped(*args, **kwargs)

@pass_through
class Metric(object):
    __metaclass__ = abc.ABCMeta

class ItemwiseMetric(Metric):
    __metaclass__ = abc.ABCMeta
```

### Error Message
```
$ python2.7 /tmp/i1.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/i1.py", line 15, in <module>
    class ItemwiseMetric(Metric):
  File "/opt/cr/lib/python2.7/abc.py", line 87, in __new__
    cls = super(ABCMeta, mcls).__new__(mcls, name, bases, namespace)
TypeError: Error when calling the metaclass bases
    metaclass conflict: the metaclass of a derived class must be a (non-strict) subclass of the metaclasses of all its bases
```

issue_title: Wrong folder scanned for group/host_vars
issue_body:
At these lines https://github.com/fboender/ansible-cmdb/blob/master/src/ansiblecmdb/ansible.py#L126 and https://github.com/fboender/ansible-cmdb/blob/master/src/ansiblecmdb/ansible.py#L177, the path shown in the debug message doesn't match the one used for the rest of the method.

This leads to inconsistent behavior: if I pass an inventory without a trailing slash (eg. `-i /home/user/inventories/inventory-prod`), it logs "Parsing host vars (dir): /home/user/inventories/inventory-prod/group_vars", but the `path` is `/home/user/inventories/group_vars`.

I tried a quick fix, changing these lines to something like
```python
path = os.path.join(inventory_path, 'group_vars')
self.log.debug("Parsing group vars (dir): {0}".format(path))
```
fixes the issue, while also not doing the "almost same but wrong sometimes" calculation in debug print and in variable affectation.

issue_title: Failing red tests, possibly with exceptions
issue_body:
Hi, 
Thank you for this great plugin, I'm finding it very useful for quality assurance on tests.
I would like to put in a feature request for having an option to fail tests if they exceed the warning threshold. 

In other words, 'red' or 'error' classifications will be failed with an error message like "All the assertions in this test passed, but the test itself was slower than the allowed threshold of <n> seconds". 

Along with this, it would be nice to have the option to pass in the python path of certain tests that should be excluded from failing based on time. For example parameters like 
    --ignore-time-for-test=tests.MyTestClass.test_this --ignore-time-for-test=tests.MyTestClass2.test_this2
This would be useful if we have a good reason why some tests should run for longer than the normal threshold. 

I would be happy to make contributions to implement the above.

issue_title: When saving a revision, automatically create an initial revision if there's none.
issue_body:
General motivation: don't save unnecessary revisions to save database space and time (CPU, DB access).

It can be a good match for data that is not edited really often but that can be edited manually.

Examples :

1. Blog entries, many entries are never edited after the creation.
2. Legacy data already in the database (before reversion was active).
3. Legacy data imported from elsewhere (old DB, CSV, etc.).
4. Data coming automatically from an external source (aggregator, parser, etc.), could be at high speed so less CPU and DB access is an advantage.

I implemented it here: https://github.com/etienned/django-reversion/commit/b0b4f4bf498f6438ab5e66f39c1f2b2dc60b0906

Commits after that are mostly optimisations. I'm really not sure that one of the last (lazily connecting auto_initial pre_save signals) is worth the trouble. But it prevent to connect the `pre_save` signal to model that don't use the auto_initial feature.

Someone interested in having this feature added to reversion?

issue_title: current_user is stored as AnonymousUserMixin when passed as argument to MagicMock
issue_body:
If I have a view function that passes current_user to another function `bar`, and a test patches `bar` with a MagicMock, `bar` will store `current_user` as `AnonymousUserMixin`.

Prior to the view function returning, current_user will have the correct value, and the MagicMock will show the correct value under call_args. After the view function returns, back in the scope of the test function, the MagicMock's value for call_args will have changed to AnonymousUserMixin.

Minimal reproduction of the bug provided here: https://github.com/brandones/flask-login-bug-minimal-repro

issue_title: Update the client and API to take advantage of the TestRail `get_results_for_run` endpoint
issue_body:
The `testrail-python` code currently only utilizes one of the available end points for collecting results:
(from client.py)
```
    # Result Methods
    def results(self, test_id):
        return map(Result, self.api.results(test_id))
```

(from api.py)
```
    # Result Requests
    def results(self, test_id):
        if self._refresh(self._results[test_id]['ts']):
            _results = self._get('get_results/%s' % test_id)
            self._results[test_id]['value'] = _results
            self._results[test_id]['ts'] = datetime.now()
        return self._results[test_id]['value']
```

This is very inefficient if you are trying to collect all results across a series of runs. TestRail offers a `get_results_for_run` endpoint that can return bulk results per run:
http://docs.gurock.com/testrail-api2/reference-results#get_results_for_run

Update the client and API to accept either a test or run, and return results accordingly.

issue_title: Horizontal scaling and publishing to a SID
issue_body:
The notes on horizontal scaling say (**emphasis mine**):
> If multiple Socket.IO servers are connected to the same message queue, **they automatically communicate with each other and manage a combined client list**, without any need for additional configuration. When a load balancer such as nginx is used, **this provides virtually unlimited scaling capabilities for the server**.

I'm concerned that emit'ing to "SID rooms" is expensive. Here's my scenario where I'm handling and transforming the pub/sub of events for individual clients:
* two clients subscribe to an event: `user:1:updated` and one request fields: `[id, name]` where the other requests `[id, name, location, friends: {id, name}]`
* i can manage the relationship between SIDs and event subscriptions at the process level
* when publishing the data to a SID room, other processes are notified of the message even if they're not hosting the SID (right?).

This reflects the current approach of GraphQL subscription frameworks (specific clients can request specific formats). My understanding then is that if I have 20k clients evenly split across 4 processes all subscribed to 1 event, each process will emit 5k messages, and be interrupted by irrelevant data 15k times.

I'm looking at the [`AsyncRedisManager`](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/master/socketio/asyncio_redis_manager.py#L72) and the [`Server`](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/master/socketio/server.py#L220) that [`AsyncServer`](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/master/socketio/asyncio_server.py#L108) subclasses from.

If I understand this right, SocketIO is unable to scale horizontally when publishing primarily to SIDs with the current implementation. Is this right?

issue_title: Substitutions in External File Flagged as Error
issue_body:
If an rST project has substitutions in an external file, restructuredtext-lint flags substitutions in the current file as an error. For example, in the attached illustration, the file `installing.txt` has some substitutions in an external file `substitutions.tsr`. The sphinx build is clean, so this substitution really isn't an error. However, the linter reports `[restructuredtext-lint] Undefined substitution referenced: "emdash".`

![rst-linter](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/24228071/28573811-dd193970-7119-11e7-9ddb-a44f7abd2281.png)

Possible resolutions:

* Linter checks if a substitution is defined somewhere in the rST project
* Include an option to disable checks for undefined substitutions.

Thank you.

issue_title: Django 1.7 deprecates declared_fieldsets
issue_body:
I am using appdata in Django 1.7 where declared_fieldsets are now deprecated and will be removed in 1.9. So I tried to use fieldsets directly and it seems that it is working correctly (there is a note in README about validation bug in Django admin) but I don't have enough experiences with Django internals to be 100% sure if it's really ok. If it's true, AppDataAdminMixin._get_form_factory_opts() method can be changed like this:

```python
def _get_form_factory_opts(self, request, obj=None, **kwargs):
    fields = flatten_fieldsets(self.get_fieldsets(request, obj))
    # ...
```

I have a use case where I select some modules from MultipleChoiceField and based on this selection, I hide or display appropriate fieldsets items and this works with get_fieldsets() only.

I could prepare a pull request but I need a little help with:

* this could break the compatibility with older Django versions so it needs to detect the version and use the old or the new way. But something like this (pseudocode) does not smell very well to me, any ideas?

```python
if version >= 1.7:
    # new way
else:
    # old way
```

* Django uses this code to get all fields from fieldsets:

```python
def get_form(self, request, obj=None, **kwargs):
    """
    Returns a Form class for use in the admin add view. This is used by
    add_view and change_view.
    """
    if 'fields' in kwargs:
        fields = kwargs.pop('fields')
    else:
        fields = flatten_fieldsets(self.get_fieldsets(request, obj))
```
appdata does not check fields in kwargs, so I am not sure, if it should be checked or not.

issue_title: Don't raise new exception
issue_body:
https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/management/email_notifications.py#L79

the raise e part is causing it to lose the exception information. It should be changed to just raise.

```
    def execute(self, *args, **options):
        """Overriden in order to send emails on unhandled exception.

        If an unhandled exception in ``def handle(self, *args, **options)``
        occurs and `--email-exception` is set or `self.email_exception` is
        set to True send an email to ADMINS with the traceback and then
        reraise the exception.

        """
        try:
            super(EmailNotificationCommand, self).execute(*args, **options)
        except Exception as e:
            if (options.get('email_exception', False) or getattr(self, 'email_exception', False)):
                self.send_email_notification(include_traceback=True)
            raise e
```

to 

```
    def execute(self, *args, **options):
        """Overriden in order to send emails on unhandled exception.

        If an unhandled exception in ``def handle(self, *args, **options)``
        occurs and `--email-exception` is set or `self.email_exception` is
        set to True send an email to ADMINS with the traceback and then
        reraise the exception.

        """
        try:
            super(EmailNotificationCommand, self).execute(*args, **options)
        except Exception:
            if (options.get('email_exception', False) or getattr(self, 'email_exception', False)):
                self.send_email_notification(include_traceback=True)
            raise
```

http://www.ianbicking.org/blog/2007/09/re-raising-exceptions.html
https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/errors.html

By raising 'e' you're causing it to lose all that valuable traceback information and all you get is an error occured line 79 of email_notification.py

By raising without 'e' you get the full traceback.

issue_title: Pipenv doesn't work well with the way install_requires is populated
issue_body:
Using pipenv to install packages that include both thrift-sasl and impyla for python3 while 'python' on the host system is python2 ends up with pipenv bailing because it ends up looking for a thrift satisfying "<=0.9.3,>=0.10.0" - which can't exist.

While pipenv's handling of sys.version_info could probably be better, a small change to setup.py fixes the issue and hopefully can be included in future releases (it's tiny, but I can create a PR if that would be better):

--- ../setup.py.orig	2017-10-12 12:39:03.000000000 -0400
+++ setup.py	2018-04-17 13:22:48.000000000 -0400
@@ -32,14 +32,18 @@
 
 # Apache Thrift does not yet support Python 3 (see THRIFT-1857).  We use
 # thriftpy as a stopgap replacement
-reqs = ['six', 'bitarray']
+reqs = [
+        'six',
+        'bitarray',
+        'thrift<=0.9.3;python_version<"3.0"',
+        'thriftpy>=0.3.5;python_version>="3.0"'
+        ]
+
 if PY2:
     packages = find_packages()
-    reqs.append('thrift<=0.9.3')
 elif PY3:
     packages = find_packages(exclude=['impala._thrift_gen',
                                       'impala._thrift_gen.*'])
-    reqs.append('thriftpy>=0.3.5')
 
 
 import versioneer  # noqa

issue_title: Default value of Union is not respected
issue_body:
Ran into this today: it seems the specified default value for ``traitlets.Union`` is not respected.

```python
import traitlets as T

class Foo(T.HasTraits):
    unicode = T.Unicode(allow_none=True, default_value=None)
    unicode_or_list = T.Union([T.Unicode(), T.List(T.Unicode())],
                              allow_none=True, default_value=None)
    
f = Foo()
print(f.unicode)  # This is None as expected
print(f.unicode_or_list)  # This is an empty string, should be None?
```

The workaround is to specify the default value within the list of items, but I found this behavior surprising. Is it a bug?

issue_title: Dict.validate_elements bug
task.complete() for BigqueryTarget
issue_body:
Trying to link a BiqueryRunQueryTask to a luigi.Task that stores data on GCS.

The upstream Join(BigqueryRunQueryTask) joins two tables and stores data back to Bigquery 

```python
def output(self):
    BigqueryTarget(..)

@property
def query(self):
    return ...
```
The downstream GCSTask(luigi.Task) has the following structure
```python
@requires(Join)
GCSTask(luigi.task):

def run(self):
input = self.input
job = {..}
bq_client = input.client
bq_client.run_job(..)
````

Technically this works fine. However, I want to use the WriteDisposition.WRITE_TRUNCATE setting, in order to override the join-table that results from the first task (in case new data comes in). 
For this it appears that I have to set BigqueryTarget.exists() to False because otherwise the BigqueryRunQueryTask will do nothing. If I do that however, the second GCSTask will never run, because self.input refers to the same BigqueryTarget and luigi.task.complete() checks whether the Target exists or not. Since I set this to False to allow for overriding the table in the first task I cannot run the workflow.
Does anybody have an idea how to go around this?

issue_title: OptimizeMeshes does not assign a name for a merged mesh
issue_body:
When OptimizeMeshes merges smaller meshes together, it doesn't assign a new name for it.

https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/code/OptimizeMeshes.cpp#L196

For me, this causes problems when combined with OptimizeGraph. After OptimizeGraph I'm left with only a couple of aiNodes, which contain multiple aiMeshes. ObjExporter then names groups with the following formula (simplified): `node.mName + _ + mesh.mName`. Since all mNames are empty, I get multiple groups with identical names, like `g $MergedNode_0.`

I think that OptimizeMeshes should at very least use a number as a `mesh.mName`

issue_title: Closing a StreamingBody does not close the socket
issue_body:
The `botocore.response.StreamingBody` (https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/response.py#L29) class defines a close method. However, closing a `StreamingBody` does not result in closing the socket. I verified this behavior by looking at packet traces after making a request for a large object in S3. Here is a code snippet to reproduce the behavior:
```
import boto3
import time

client = boto3.client('s3')
resp = client.get_object(Bucket='test', Key='large')
resp['Body'].close()
time.sleep(10)
```

The `RST` packet should be sent after calling `close()`. Currently, I see it sent 10 seconds later, when the program exits (and a number of ZeroWindow messages in between).

It appears that to close the socket, boto should also call `release_conn` on the `_raw_stream`. After adding that change, the connection is closed immediately (`RST -> FIN -> FIN`).

issue_title: Expose a tm.commit_veto2 api that passes in the exception object.
issue_body:
A fault in `tm.commit_veto` is that it does not let you evaluate the exception object when writing the veto logic. I don't see a good way to do this with the current api so I propose a `tm.commit_veto2` that adds a third parameter to the callback. Both vetos would be used if defined, as an "or" operation. So if either callback wanted to veto, then it would be vetoed.

This is useful for some people who may not want certain exceptions to cancel a transaction (e.g. `HTTPFound`).

issue_title: Possible leak in double-booking cursor
issue_body:
Probably 70fae1f98bb7 made a double acounting of region-clients in [`smmap.mman.py#L136`](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/smmap/blob/master/smmap/mman.py#L136), where a "manual" `increment_client_count()` follows a `_obtain_region()` which it also increments the client-count.

From *gitdb* TC `gitdb.test.test_pack:TestPack.test_pack_entity()` I can see that this is indeed the case, where it is increased 2 x 3 times for 3 mmaps, while it is decremented just 3 times.

issue_title: pipenv documentation for "entire construct" around entry points
issue_body:
Following up on comments made by @techalchemy in a comment here: https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/966#issuecomment-416813069

> >Anyway, going back to pipenv vs poetry - there seems to be some idea floating around that applications don't need certain features that libraries get, like entry points, and this is just incorrect. It should be straightforward for an application to be a python package.

- @mmerrickel

> Who is saying that applications don't require entry points? Pipenv has an entire construct to handle this.

- @techalchemy 

I can't find any documentation around how pipenv handles entry points. Is there somewhere in the code that I can find this? I don't mind trying my hand at a patch for the some documentation!

Thanks!

issue_title: Installing futures backport on Python 2 breaks pipenv on Python 3
issue_body:
The problem appears on systems with Python 2 installed as a default (unfortunately, will still be around for at least several years!) and `pipenv` installed on Python 2 accordingly, when users are trying to create and manage Python 3 virtualenvs.

See details about my specific setup here:

https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/1686#issuecomment-373019016

------------

##### Expected result

`pipenv` works.

##### Actual result

`pipenv` bails out with an exception:

```
zaytsev@work:~/src/pipenv$ docker run -it --rm pipenv-bug
port Link
  File "/root/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pipenv/../pipenv/vendor/pip9/index.py", line 31, in <module>
    from pip9.wheel import Wheel, wheel_ext
  File "/root/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pipenv/../pipenv/vendor/pip9/wheel.py", line 6, in <module>
    import compileall
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/compileall.py", line 20, in <module>
    from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor
  File "/root/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pipenv/../pipenv/../concurrent/futures/__init__.py", line 8, in <module>
    from concurrent.futures._base import (FIRST_COMPLETED,
  File "/root/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pipenv/../pipenv/../concurrent/futures/_base.py", line 414
    raise exception_type, self._exception, self._traceback
                        ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

##### Steps to replicate

```
# Build image
zaytsev@work:~/src/pipenv$ docker build . --tag pipenv-bug

# Reproduce bug
zaytsev@work:~/src/pipenv$ docker run -it --rm pipenv-bug

# Debug live
zaytsev@work:~/src/pipenv$ docker run -it --rm pipenv-bug /bin/sh
```

Dockerfile:

```
zaytsev@work:~/src/pipenv$ cat Dockerfile 
FROM python:2.7-alpine3.7

WORKDIR /opt

RUN apk add --no-cache python3 gcc musl-dev libffi-dev openssl-dev

RUN pip install --user pipenv

ENV PATH="/root/.local/bin:${PATH}" SHELL="/bin/sh"

RUN pipenv --python 3.6

# Installing this breaks pipenv!
RUN pip install --user futures

CMD ["pipenv", "update", "--dry-run"]

```

##### Duplicates:

* #1686
* #1603
* #1589
* #1553

issue_title: Installing dependencies but not app
issue_body:
My current setup uses a requirements.txt which lists all my dependencies (and transitive dependencies), which I basically generate from pip-tools. To deploy my app, I do `pip install -r requirements.txt` then `pip install -e .`, to install the dependencies and the app, respectively.

What is great with this system is that it works correctly with Docker, caching the dependencies and keeping my builds very fast:
```
# This part will be cached, only reinstalling if I change the requirements
ADD requirements.txt ./
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

# This part runs every time I change any of the app's code, but is fast because dependencies are installed
ADD setup.py app ./
RUN pip install -e .
```

I am not sure how to get this working with pipenv. If I `install -e ./`, my app appears in `Pipfile`, and there's no way for me to install only the dependencies. If I don't put my app in Pipfile, I need to copy all the dependencies from `setup.py` to `Pipfile`.

That makes the build for anyone using Docker hundreds of time slower, and is a huge barrier to Docker users moving to pipenv.

------------

##### Expected result

Being able to use `Pipfile` in my Docker build.

##### Actual result

Build is made very slow by the fact I can't cache installation of the dependencies.

##### Steps to replicate

This fails because `.` is not copied yet (it might even try to grab the app from Git):
```
ADD Pipfile Pipfile.lock ./
RUN pipenv install
```

------------

I am not sure what's a good fix for this, or if it's high profile enough for pipenv devs to address. A way to install dependencies but not the app would solve this. A way to have `Pipfile.lock` list the dependencies from `setup.py` without having it list the app would be another way.

Anyone else using pipenv to build applications deployed via Docker?

issue_title: Specification for virtualenv's location
issue_body:
I'm contributing to add Pipfile support Sublime/Anaconda. The biggest problem right now, is to get the virtualenv's location we need to call a subprocess for `pipenv --py`. The only alternative would be to bundle the whole 20 MB dependency what pip installing pipenv brings.

I believe this will be the same situation in every IDE which would like to implement support for Pipfiles. If Pipfile aims to be a standard like `package.json` it should provide a very simple way of finding out where is the virtualenv installed. Imagine if every JS editor would need to call a subprocess just to find where are `node_modules` for a given project.

I think the hashing, env parsing part of pipenv could be extracted in a few dozen lines of Python maximum. Later that reference implementation can also be ported to other languages, to support JS based editors for example.

issue_title: PS1 isn't reset if empty
issue_body:
If one activates a virtualenv while PS1 is empty (eg a non-login shell), then deactivates it, the environment name will be left in the prompt. This can then cause lots of confusion as scripts assume "PS1 is non-empty" == "interactive shell"

```
$ export PS1=
. myEnv/bin/activate
(myEnv) deactivate
(myEnv) # env name is still here after deactivate
```

```
$ export PS1=moo
moo . myEnv/bin/activate
(myEnv)moo deactivate
moo # env name is gone
```

add_or_replace_parameter() when URL doesn't end on slash

Is this the expected behavior for `add_or_replace_parameter`?

```python
>>> from w3lib.url import add_or_replace_parameter
>>> add_or_replace_parameter('http://example.com', 'foo', 'bar')
'http://example.com?foo=bar'
```

issue_title: Synchronous batch produce() no longer possible in 2.0?
issue_body:
This doesn't apply to a released version, just the current trunk that is slated for 2.0.  

As far as I can tell, there is currently not a reasonable way to express a synchronous batch produce(...) at the discretion of the application in the Producer interface.

Whenever sync=True, you get one-by-one synchronous publishing.  It waits with arbitrary sleeps up to 300ms for empty queues after every single message ( produce(...) only accepts one message at a time. )

If sync=False, well then ... it's not synchronous and you aren't guaranteed that a flush has taken place no matter how you adjust batching parameters / timing parameters.

There is no public method to produce a batch of messages and wait for them to all be flushed.
There is no public method to force a synchronous flush of all pending messages.

This might be planned already, not sure.  Seems like this might also be helped by https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/238

issue_title: PartitionOwnedError / ConsumerStoppedException upon Consumer startup
issue_body:

**Partitions**: 15
**Consumer**

This happens in multiple projects on multiple data centers, all with different Kafka clusters (albeit they are the same version, and all were installed and managed by the Ambari tool). 

When I start up a consumer, unless I slowly turn them on one by one, some or all will get caught in a perpetual restart loop where it is unable to start subscribing. It sees, and usually bounces back and forth between either PartitionOwnedError or ConsumerStoppedException. It shuts down and is then restarted by my Scheduler or docker-compose, where it errors again.

    consumer_group='test_group',

)

issue_title: Massive threading and concurrency issues in async
issue_body:
While investigating closure issues in async I realized it was suboptimally implemented with several concurrency issues. Firstly, there was about 3 times the amount of code than was necessary. Secondly, the `flush`, `drain` and `queue_timeout` were all added to cover over the fact that the async thread couldn't be stopped reliably.

This became a massive rewrite of the entire async handler and sender, which shrunk the code dramatically, removed queue timeout, moved flush to default (observing the expected behavior of the Handler) and removed all possible race conditions on closing and sending.

Finally virtually all tests have been rewritten and cleaned up as they were reporting failures as successes. Tests now only take a few seconds to complete due to removal of `sleep` that pretended to synchronize several threads.

Original issue description:
```
Currently async handler will flush but also will discard on close. 
These parameters need to be controlled during handler instantiation to make sure that no logs are lost.

https://github.com/fluent/fluent-logger-python/blob/master/fluent/asyncsender.py#L76
```

issue_title: Benchmark code location
issue_body:
I would like to use this thread to start a discussion on where an appropriate place for benchmarking code would be.

Performance seems to be of interest to at least a few people using scikit-image (See Issue and pull requests tagged with type: performance).

I've been trying to include my code in commit messages, or in the pull requests, but I feel like that would get lost. Having a directory for benchmarking code is useful because:

- Future developers can quickly check that a certain routine was tested for speed.
- Meaningful discussions can be had by comparing other contributor's benchmarks.
- If a decision was made due to performance, it should be documented somewhere permenant.
- When a certain algorithm is no longer the best choice, other benchmarked algorithms can quickly replace it.
- This would also allow scikit-image to establish best practices for benchmarking.
   - I've noticed differences between ipython, and jupyter for example. Maybe we want to target one and not the other?

Would a directory such a `benchmark` next to `tests` be an acceptable solution?

issue_title: `python setup.py install` with `cysignals` in `install_requires`
issue_body:
I get the following error:

https://github.com/sagemath/cysignals/blob/db075e556b8b664690e73eae4b22b80be58064e0/setup.py#L142

In my case, the package that depends on `cysignals` builds extensions if optional arguments are given to its `setup.py`. These arguments cannot be passed via `pip`'s `--install-option`, because it (unintendedly) propagates them to dependencies, raising errors (https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/1883).

So `python setup.py install` is the only option for installing from source (we could imagine `pip install .` first to get the dependencies including `cysignals`, then re-installing with `python setup.py install --foo`, but that is cumbersome).

Is there any way to have `python setup.py install` successfully install `cysignals` if it encounters it in `install_requires`?

(Assuming appropriate [environment markers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/#environment-markers) are used, for example `'cysignals >= 1.7.0; platform_system = "Linux" or platform_system = "Darwin"'`.)

issue_title: Test graceful exit in Sage
issue_body:
We disabled the following test because it does not make sense in the standalone module. However, we should make sure that this test is run in Sage once it switches to the standalone module.

```python
def test_graceful_exit():
    r"""
    Start a Sage subprocess, spawn a child PARI/GP process and kill the
    Sage process.  The PARI/GP process should exit by itself. ::

        >>> from subprocess import *
        >>> from signal import *
        >>> P = Popen(['python'], stdin=PIPE, stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE)
        >>> P.stdin.write('from signal_pyx.tests import *\n')
        >>> P.stdin.write('test_graceful_exit()\n')

    Now read from the child until we read ``"GO"``.  This ensures that
    the child Sage process has properly started before we terminate it::

        >>> while "GO" not in P.stdout.readline(): pass  # long time
        >>> os.kill(P.pid, SIGHUP)  # long time
        >>> print 'stdout =', P.stdout.read()   (1200 + 5*p + 10*n milliseconds)
        stdout = ...Exiting PARI/GP interpreter...
        >>> P.wait()  # long time
        0

    """
    # This code is executed in the subprocess
    import os, sys
    from sage.interfaces.gp import gp

    # Keep PARI/GP busy
    gp(0)  # Ensure PARI/GP is started
    gp._expect.sendline("factor(2^1000-3);")

    # Print something to synchronize with the parent
    print("GO")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    # Wait to be killed...
    sig_on()
    infinite_loop()
```

issue_title: SBP object implementation can't use slots
issue_body:
http://bugs.python.org/issue15535

As far as I can tell, if you implement a class with slots, the python pickling library will have difficulties, looking for an implicit `__dict__` attribute when none exists. Incidentally, you can add a `__dict__` attribute try and fool python, but then it'll go ahead and call `dict()` anyway (which sucks). In any case, whenever we've moved off using picking serialization for anything, we ought to use `__slots__` for the memory help, since it may actually be useful here for reducing memory usage of serial_link and the testing stuff.

For an implementation example, see: https://github.com/construct/construct/blob/master/construct/core.py#L100

issue_title: Add support for pickle protocol 4: It can be *much* faster
issue_body:
Pickle [protocol 4](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3154/), introduced with Python 3.4, brings some nice enhancements. In particular, it turns out that the [framing](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3154/#id19) feature introduced in protocol 4 "with a potentially huge performance impact" really does have a big impact. 

I'm using zodbpickle to gain access to pickle protocol 3 under Python 2 in RelStorage. Under Python 3, I use the builtin pickle and (under 3.4 and up) protocol 4.

In one test in RelStorage, protocol 4 takes about 5 seconds to handle an object, whereas protocol 3 takes about **30 seconds**. (To workaround this in Python 2, I have to buffer a large object into memory; even using buffered IO from an SSD and with the data putatively in the buffer cache. Of course, this varies by system, but the slower the filesystem/stream the worse the problem).

It would be nice to have protocol 4 available to python 2.

I don't know how much work would be involved in the backport (and making sure that no_load stays supported). I personally don't have any concrete plans to do this work right now (maybe someday), but I thought I'd throw this feature request out there along with the motivating numbers in case it intrigues any one else.

```
class JSONWebTokenSerializer(Serializer):
    """

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):

issue_title: Inject itself into sys.modules for other PyQt versions
issue_body:
Scenario:

In my app, I `from qtpy.QtCore import Qt`. I run my app with `QT_API=pyqt5`, so PyQt5 is preferred, defaulting to PyQt4 if the former is not available. Anyway, PyQt5 is available and my app runs fine.

In some obscure module, or in a stand-alone add-on for my app, I rely on a third-party Python package that imports and works with PyQt4. The import fails. My app breaks.

Use case:

I would like like qtpy to inject itself into `sys.modules` so that any requests for objects from PyQt4 are resolved, _best effort_, from qtpy.

issue_title: Integration with system fonts
issue_body:
With the new release of QtAwesome v4.x, patching the code to use the system fonts produces a `FontError`, most likely due to a mismatch between the mdf5 sums of the vendored fonts compared to the system ones. Please remember that vendoring is *strongly* discouraged by Linux distributions, hence the need for an integration path.

I would like to discuss with you guys what the best course of actions would be to have `QtAwesome` work with system fonts without complicating the package maintenance burden. Shall, I just make a patch to disable the sum checking part of the code?

issue_title: should the Python module's version always match the library version?
issue_body:
[v0.2.0](https://github.com/google/brotli/releases/tag/v0.2.0) has been tagged, but the Python package version as stored in `python/brotlimodule.cc` still says "0.1.0" (see [here](https://github.com/google/brotli/blob/master/python/brotlimodule.cc#L12)).
This version string is read by the setup.py, and stored in the package metadata upon installing.

I wonder if the Python module's version should also increase every time a Brotli update is tagged, or if language bindings should have their own version numbers, independent from the core library.

WDYT?

issue_title: Spikecount cannot be estimated when signal at the end of the trace is larger than the threshold
issue_body:
This was reported by @sasaray.

First trace gives Spikecount 3 while second trace returns Spikecount 0.
When the signal at the end of the trace is larger than the threshold, Spikecount and possibly other features cannot be estimated. This seems to be independent of stim_end.

![figure_1](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5420759/17175365/75b79702-5407-11e6-9053-94b126236b21.png)

```
import numpy
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import efel

dt = 0.1
v = numpy.zeros(int(100/dt)) - 70.0
v[int(20/dt):int(25/dt)] = 20.
v[int(40/dt):int(45/dt)] = 20.
v[int(60/dt):int(65/dt)] = 20.

traces = []
trace = {}
trace['T'] = numpy.arange(len(v))*dt
trace['V'] = v
trace['stim_start'] = [10]
trace['stim_end'] = [50]
traces.append(trace)

plt.subplot(2,1,1)
plt.plot(trace['T'], trace['V'])

traces_results = efel.getFeatureValues(traces,['Spikecount'])
print traces_results

# When the signal at the end of the trace is larger than the threshold, Spikecount and possibly other features cannont be estimated.
v[int(80/dt):] = -19

traces_results = efel.getFeatureValues(traces,['Spikecount'])
print traces_results

plt.subplot(2,1,2)
plt.plot(trace['T'], trace['V'])
plt.show()
```

issue_title: Easier-to-understand naming for LSTMBuilder
issue_body:
LSTMBuilder currently represents a relatively unorthodox implementation of LSTMs using coupled input and forget gates: http://dynet.readthedocs.io/en/latest/unorthodox.html#lstm-implementation

I think very few people will realize this, and this could have a significant impact on results (for example, the DyNet LSTMBuilder will never have a cell state with abs(c)>1, but vanilla LSTMs can). It'd probably be best to have the basic LSTMBuilder represent something that looks more like what people expect, so I propose the following change:

* Rename LSTMBuilder to CoupledLSTMBuilder
* Make LSTMBuilder a typedef for VanillaLSTMBuilder

If there aren't any objections, maybe we'll do this in the upcoming 1.1 release: https://github.com/clab/dynet/projects

issue_title: Dealiasing VPT data
issue_body: @jjhelmus and @scollis from a past email I mentioned that the phase unwrap algorithm had difficulties with the insect and shallow cumulus layer, but I now have firm evidence that both the new algorithms have issues with this layer. Here's a [link](http://1drv.ms/1OjK7L4) to a full days worth of KAZR data that I have processed using `dealias_region_based`, where I have used the default settings. This shallow layer is incorrectly dealiased for about 20 hours, where at approximately 2033 UTC and afterwards the results improve. The merging cloud near this time almost certainly has something to do with it, however between 10-11 UTC a higher altitude cloud merges with this layer and results also deteriorate.

issue_title: Py-ART ideas from the 2015 AMS radar conference
issue_body:
This is a meta-issue for keeping track of ideas that were discussed with Py-ART users and supporters at the 2015 AMS radar conference.  Expect this top level comment to be edited to reflect changes as development progressed.  Some of these issue already have their own issue in the tracker, I'll add links to these when possible.  

Please provide any additions to this list.  Feel free to share opinions as to the priority of any of these ideas.

### Idea for Py-ART from the conference broken down by area

#### pyart.io

* [x] UF writing (#186, completed in #388) 
* [ ] Reading NEXRAD Level II message 1 format (#352)
* [ ] Reading single field/sweep from NEXRAD Level II
* [ ] Interpolating NEXRAD data to a uniform grid spacing when not-constant (#152, #252)
* [ ] Full support for reading ODIM_H5 files (#131)
* [ ] Writing ODIM_H5 files.

#### pyart.correct

* [ ] Correct unfolded velocities in the region based dealiasing algorithm using a sonde profile or previously unfolded field.
* [ ] Updated phase processing code using self consistency or other additional constraints (#125)

#### pyart.graph
* [ ] Default colormaps based on field name.
* [ ] Default luminosity limits based on field name.
* [ ] Categorical colorbars.
* [ ] Expose calculation of inputs to pcolormesh in the  public API.

#### pyart.grid
* [ ] Three dimensional linear interpolation based gridding as used by SPRINT.
* [ ] Nearest neighbor gridding (#182)
* [ ] Distance weighted gridding where the weighting distance in x, y and z can be specified independently 

#### pyart.retrieve
* [ ] QPE equaltion, R(A), R(KDP), etc.
* [ ] VAD, VVT and DVAD retrievals.  Some of these are available in [SingleDop](https://github.com/nasa/SingleDop)

#### Other 
* [ ] Quasi vertical profiles, QVP.
* [ ] Object for iteration over radar objects by specifying a list of files (useful for QVP).
* [X] Cross section tool for making building RHI like scans from PPI data (completed in #380)
* [ ] Radar composibility (add, remove, insert sweeps or rays) (related #127 and #242)
* [ ] Create conda packages for other radar tools (RSL, wradlib, RadX, PyTDA, SingleDop, ARTView, etc).
* [ ] GridFilter class.
* [ ] GateFilter method to include/exclude based upon height of gate.

#### Documenation
* [ ] Add links to short course materials.

issue_title: BigQuery: add page_size parameter for list_rows and use in DB-API for arraysize
issue_body:
With https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/pull/4144 the arraysize parameter no longer affects how much data the library actually requests from the API.

We should add a `page_size` parameter to `Client.list_rows` and use that parameter in the DB-API implementation.

See also: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/4134

issue_title: bigquery: please support non-legacy-sql nested data type, and preserve order in json
issue_body:
report in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-node/issues/1593#issuecomment-247506295 from a nodejs app, but since my app can also be written in Python I have a test of Python BigQuery API as well, it seems have a same problem:

```console
$ bq.py --format=prettyjson query --nouse_legacy_sql 'SELECT ARRAY[STRUCT("zx83" AS id, 1 AS a, 2 AS b), ("f8f7", 4, 7)] d' |& cat -An
...
     2	[$
     3	  {$
     4	    "d": [$
     5	      {$
     6	        "a": "1", $
     7	        "b": "2", $
     8	        "id": "zx83"$
     9	      }, $
    10	      {$
    11	        "a": "4", $
    12	        "b": "7", $
    13	        "id": "f8f7"$
    14	      }$
    15	    ]$
    16	  }$
    17	]$
```

the problems:

1. nested value types are not interpreted, the value types of a and b can be inferred as Integers (from bigquery api response, there are schema including nested field's data type), should be converted to python integers; (the way to fix will need somewhat recursively interpret data types)
2. keys order are not preserved, someone here might argue following reasons but there are ways workaround:
    1. JSON object's key order doesn't matter to machine; but when we print a prettyjson format, it's for **human, and keys order does matter**
    2. python's default `json.loads` convert a JSON object to python dict which uses key's internal hash code somewhat unpredictable order, way to fix is to load with `OrderedDict`, and drop sort_keys parameter when calling `json.dumps`
3. trailing space in object key value pair lines, this is trivial and some Python JSON API's own problem, there are many careless python doing this including this project; please read below expected output, can be fixed by `json.dumps(..., separators=(',', ': '))`

many tools written in Python suffered the same problem, like what I commented in the popular httpie tool: https://github.com/jkbrzt/httpie/issues/427#issuecomment-247663237

## HOW to Fix 2 & 3

```py
In [1]: import json

In [2]: from collections import OrderedDict

In [3]: data = json.loads(
             '{"d": [ { "id": "zx83", "a": 1, "b": 2}, { "id": "f8f7", "a": 4, "b": 7 }  ]}',
               object_pairs_hook=OrderedDict)

In [4]: data
Out[4]: 
OrderedDict([(u'd',
              [OrderedDict([(u'id', u'zx83'), (u'a', 1), (u'b', 2)]),
               OrderedDict([(u'id', u'f8f7'), (u'a', 4), (u'b', 7)])])])

In [5]: json.dumps(data, indent=2, separators=(',', ': '))
Out[5]: '{\n  "d": [\n    {\n      "id": "zx83",\n      "a": 1,\n      "b": 2\n    },\n    {\n      "id": "f8f7",\n      "a": 4,\n      "b": 7\n    }\n  ]\n}'

In [6]: print json.dumps(data, indent=2, separators=(',', ': '))
{
  "d": [
    {
      "id": "zx83",
      "a": 1,
      "b": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "f8f7",
      "a": 4,
      "b": 7
    }
  ]
}
```

Expected output:

```console
     2	[$
     3	  {$
     4	    "d": [$
     5	      {$
     6	        "id": "zx83",$
     7	        "a": 1,$
     8	        "b": 2$
     9	      },$
    10	      {$
    11	        "id": "f8f7",$
    12	        "a": 4,$
    13	        "b": 7$
    14	      }$
    15	    ]$
    16	  }$
    17	]$
```

issue_title: Google PubSub Subscription stops pulling messages from topic.
issue_body:
In response to issue [5444 - Pubsub 0.35.3 stop consuming messages](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/5444#issuecomment-400500096) and per theacodes request I am opening up a new issue.

I am currently running a container on kubernetes with 40 pods that are pulling from a subscription that is receiving ~50 messages a second. I am currently running 35.4

This is my code that i modified from the documentation that is pulling the messages.

```
def main():
    #Receives messages from a pull subscription.
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(project, subscription_name)

    def callback(message):
        log.info('Received message: {}'.format(message))
        try:
            ret = bl.process_message(message)
            if ret.status_code == 200:
                log.info('200 File processed')
                statsd.increment('data-log-file-processed')
                message.ack()
            else:
                log.error('Could not process message for data log file publisher. {}' .format(ret.message))
                message.ack()
        except Exception as e:
            log.error('Could not process message: {} error occurred. {}' .format(ret.message, e))
    subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path, callback=callback)

    # The subscriber is non-blocking, so we must keep the main thread from
    # exiting to allow it to process messages in the background.
    log.info('Listening for messages on {}'.format(subscription_path))
    while True:
        time.sleep(60)
```

The SubsciberClient will pull messages for a time than just stop pulling them. After putting the logs to debug mode i am seeing the following:

[Log File](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/11DnGk_HchcCwOH_vKnd_UnZqs25qWAT4nDqoyDQjdqk/edit?usp=sharing)

Line 97 is the last message I received after that it seems to just go into an endless loop without ever pulling any messages. I know there are more messages to be pulled because my other pods are still pulling messages from the topic.

Please let me know if there is anything else needed to get this fixed or if it is something I am doing wrong. Thanks!

merge with different domains
issue_body:
It could be useful to be able to merge time series with different domains. The resulting domain could either be the union or intersection. In case of the union, it would probably be necessary to specify a "fill value".

This would be useful if you happen to have time series with different domains (say, light bulb sensors but with different on times), and you still want to do a simple count of "number of lights on".

```
TimeSeries.merge([a, b], domain='union', fillvalue=0) 
TimeSeries.merge([a, b], domain='intersection')
TimeSeries.merge([a, b], domain=None) # throw error with different domains
```

issue_title: pex cannot resolve local dependencies with dash in the name
issue_body:
pex does not work well with a local pypi. So I followed the work around described here https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/issues/270 . However, if you have dependencies with a dash in the name like javaobj-py3 or asn1-modules, pex fails. The following demonstrates the issue.

Note in the following example, pip is configured to use a local pypi like so:

```
[root@8d56519da316 /]# cat /root/.pip/pip.conf
[global]
index-url = https://my-local-pypi/pypi/simple
```

First download the wheel:
```
pip wheels -w wheels javaobj-py3==0.2.3
ERROR: unknown command "wheels" - maybe you meant "wheel"
[root@4d3e53cc5bc5 newdir]# pip wheel -w wheels javaobj-py3==0.2.3
Collecting javaobj-py3==0.2.3
  Downloading https://my-local-pypi/pypi/simple/javaobj-py3/javaobj-py3-0.2.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl
  Saved ./wheels/javaobj-py3-0.2.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Skipping javaobj-py3, due to already being wheel.
```

Now try creating a pex file with it.
```
pex --no-index --repo=wheels -o this_wont_work.pex javaobj-py3==0.2.3
Could not satisfy all requirements for javaobj-py3==0.2.3:
    javaobj-py3==0.2.3
```

Here is a workaround which will create a hard link to all python modules and replace the a dash with underscore.

```
ln wheels/javaobj-py3-0.2.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl wheels/javaobj_py3-0.2.3-py2.py3-none-any.whl
pex --no-index --repo=wheels -o this_workaround_works.pex javaobj-py3==0.2.3
[root@4d3e53cc5bc5 newdir]# pex --no-index --repo=wheels -o this_workaround_works.pex javaobj-py3==0.2.3
[root@4d3e53cc5bc5 newdir]# ls -la
total 464
drwxr-xr-x. 1 root root     62 May  1 23:42 .
drwxrwxrwt. 1 root root     26 May  1 23:42 ..
-rwxr-xr-x. 1 root root 471703 May  1 23:42 this_workaround_works.pex
drwxr-xr-x. 1 root root    152 May  1 23:26 wheels
```

issue_title: Inlining of PathGlob expansion causes excess invalidation with the daemon
issue_body:
Inlining of `PathGlobsExpansion` causes `Snapshots` to be more fragile than necessary. In particular, because `VFS::expand` recursively walks from the root down to the relevant directories without memoizing `PathGlobsExpansion`s, any change in the root invalidates _all_ `Snapshots` from a changed path up to the root of the repo (but not the directory listings and stats).

This significantly affects warm performance in the presence of the daemon.

We should experiment with memoizing (portions of?) `PathGlobsExpansion` in the `Graph` (similar to what we do for `ReadLink`/`Scandir`), and see how much it affects cold/warm performance.

NB: As a wildcard: it's possible that fixing #4558 would be a better approach than increasing memoization would be.

----

The relevant codepath centers around https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/cdba694c38b4d60342c75a44ff97c5642429e585/src/rust/engine/fs/src/lib.rs#L570-L576 . When we call that method from the engine (here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/01bcda6e3c352d99dc6a583ed57295d3a4c5afe8/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L913-L940), the `VFS` in question is https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/01bcda6e3c352d99dc6a583ed57295d3a4c5afe8/src/rust/engine/src/nodes.rs#L73 , which has the effect of memoizing/recording `ReadLink` and `Scandir` calls in the `Graph` (which later allows for filesystem invalidation).

The effect of memoizing `ReadLink` and `Scandir` (but none of the rest of the other steps of a `PathGlobsExpansion`) is that a `Snapshot` `Node` in the `Graph` is flat: the `Snapshot` depends directly on the syscalls, and to recompute it the entire `VFS::expand` call reruns.

----

When we fix this, it would be good to get a simple benchmark in place on the rust side. But it's also important to directly measure the performance of:
* cold `./pants list ::`
* warm `./pants list ::` after touching one file at the root of the repo

issue_title: Inline execution of Dependencies/Projection Node
issue_body:
Currently, DependenciesNode and ProjectionNode are always created and stored in the ProductGraph. This has the effect of memoizing/caching their execution, and 99% of the time this is overkill. It helps to have the Nodes in the graph for debugging purposes, but we're not interested in caching or threading for them, let alone multiprocessing.

A simple transform to not actually create Nodes that we won't cache/memoize involves recursively inlining the `step` method on non-task/non-FS dependency Nodes. But there is a question of whether (since in those cases they're never persisted in the ProductGraph) the intermediate Nodes should even be Node subclasses.

issue_title: Implement exports literal
issue_body:
In cases where public APIs of targets guarantee the need for some other target, it is sometimes helpful/necessary to be able to "export" a dependency to consuming targets.

Implement `exports` literal in pants to be able to specify which of a target's dependencies will be exported so the consumers would not need to specify/know about these.

An example of this usage would be:
 
```
java_library(
  name='A',
  dependencies=[
    'src/java/dep/it:needs',
  ]
  exports=[
    'src/java/dep/it:needs',
  ]
)
```

issue_title: Android: handle JDK compatibilty
issue_body:
Android is purposefully not strictly compatible with any one version of Java. As of right now, some sdks are only supported by JDK6, while later SDKs support both 6 and 7.  This can get sticky, as some code can be compiled by newer JDKs (e.g. 1.8) but will choke the dx.jar later in the build process.

The compile and dex phases need to be able to at least serve a decent error message. Passing things like minimum_version and maximum_version to the Nailgun would be even better.

issue_title: Cache ./pants bootstrapping in CI using stages
issue_body: As @wisechengyi noted in [@pants-devel](https://groups.google.com/d/topic/pants-devel/_vEJ0F_190U/discussion), CI now spends ~10 minutes per shard bootstrapping pants. This time is largely spent compiling the native engine and so the time will only grow as more of Pants moves to Rust. As a stop-gap to Pants growing and then dogfooding Rust support such that we could use Pants own distributed cache (seems like this might be doable someday), leverage Travis-CI build stages to 1st build a pants pex, upload it to S3 and then use that pex in parallel on test shards. An additional benefit to this setup is that we can have a fully vetted pants pex as a release target instead of a pex built after-the-fact (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/6014).

issue_title: Error handling when wrong sample CI requested.
issue_body: Currently, when we request wrong sample CI it throws the exception. An error message with valid Resource Type format would be more helpful.

issue_title: can we do GetCrossReferenceItems?
issue_body:
Currently, I do the work by using com, which looks like
1. In word, Auto-numbered item can be inserted and then read by objDoc.GetCrossReferenceItems(win32com.client.constants.wdRefTypeNumberedItem)
( https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/aa172208%28v=office.11%29.aspx)

2. then we can find which auto-numbered item is cross-refered by judging whether its type is wdFieldRef, just as https://www.datanumen.com/blogs/3-quick-ways-find-fields-word-document/ does

com does work, but it is some slow. So my question is can we do this work with python-docx, and how? If not, is there any other programmable method? thanks

issue_title: Creating index entries
issue_body: I am creating a large (200+ pages) document using python-docx, and I need to create an index of all names in the document. I understand that Word marks index entries a using special XE (Index Entry) field that includes the marked main entry, e.g. { XE indexed-name }. Is it possible to do this using python-docx? I would be happy to create the actual index manually at the end, as long as the index entries are generated automatically.

issue_title: How to insert a paragraph to a specific section?
issue_body:
Since python-docx can’t add or change things like headers or footnotes yet, but if the document has them python-docx is polite enough to leave them alone and smart enough to save them without actually understanding what they are.

So I make a "template.docx" file include searval sections. Different sections have different headers and footers. The question is "how can I insert a paragraph or a picture to specific section?"

issue_title: Issue using Pillow in a threaded context
issue_body: I'm reading a bunch of JPG files from multiple threads at the same time. I consistently get `"Imageio Pillow plugin requires Pillow lib."` from `imageio.plugins.pillow.PillowFormat._init_pillow()`. If I wrap everything inside `_init_pillow()` in a lock, it works fine. Any idea how to make sure that `_init_pillow()` was called without reading a fake JPG file?
