issue_title: Put a toggle to make a worksheet/bundle private
improve on metrics script
apply_similar() and create_similar() should import to vsys1 for interfaces with no vsys
issue_body:
In order to mimic the behavior of the firewall GUI which prevents interfaces without any vsys, the `create()` and `apply()` methods check for the `VsysOperations.ALWAYS_IMPORT` constant.  If the constant is True, then the PanObject is imported into vsys1.  All interfaces have the ALWAYS_IMPORT constant set to True.  However, `apply_similar()` and `create_similar()` don't make this check, so the interfaces created by these methods end up without any vsys.

To correct this, `PanObject._requires_import_consideration()` should check for the `ALWAYS_IMPORT` constant, but since this constant exists in `VsysOperations` class, not `PanObject` class, the `_requires_import_consideration()` method should probably be moved to the `VsysOperations` class.

The `PanObject._perform_vsys_dict_import_set()` method should also probably be in the `VsysOperations` class, since it is specific to vsys importable objects.

issue_title: ExaBGP unconditionally sets TCP_MD5SIG sockopt on Linux.
issue_body:
<!---
Hello,

We are sorry that you are experiencing an issue with ExaBGP.

Before opening this issue could you please:
 - make sure the problem was not already reported to avoid duplicates.
 - check if the problem is still present on the latest version
   using the 3.4 or master branch (if you know what it means)
   alternatively the latest release (3.4 or 4.0)

For us to help you the outpout of exabgp -d is often required.
Please use ``` for any copied output.
-->

##### Summary
ExaBGP 4.0.6 unconditionally sets socket to TCP_MD5SIG, even if MD5 isn't in use.



##### OS
Linux 4.17.3 WITHOUT CONFIG_TCP_MD5SIG

```
Linux host1.example.com 4.17.3-36.el7 #1 SMP Fri Jun 29 19:15:06 UTC 2018 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

##### Version
<!--
Paste verbatim the output from “exabgp --version” between quotes below
-->
```
ExaBGP : 4.0.6-daa3d6ba
Python : 3.6.3 (default, Mar 20 2018, 13:50:41)  [GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)]
Uname  : Linux host.example.com 4.17.3-36.el7 #1 SMP Fri Jun 29 19:15:06 UTC 2018 x86_64
Root   : /opt/rh/rh-python36/root/usr
```

##### Installation
<!--
Please let us know how you installed ExaBGP 
-->
```
Custom RPM Package
```


##### Program output
<!-- 
When encountering a problem please run exabgp with the "-d" option

THIS IS THE MOST IMPORTANT INFORMATION - WITHOUT IT WE ARE UNLIKELY TO BE ABLE TO HELP
-->
```
17:44:37 | 410288 | outgoing-1    | outgoing-1 2620:xxxx:43:c103::5-2620:149:43:c103::1, closing connection
17:44:37 | 410288 | outgoing-1    | connection to 2620:xxxx:43:c103::1:179 failed, This linux machine does not support TCP_MD5SIG, you can not use MD5 ([Errno ENOPROTOOPT] [Errno 92] Protocol not available)
17:44:37 | 410288 | reactor       | initialising connection to peer-1
17:44:37 | 410288 | outgoing-1    | attempting connection to 2620:xxxx:43:c103::1:179
17:44:37 | 410288 | outgoing-1    | outgoing-1 2620:xxxx:43:c103::5-2620:xxxx:43:c103::1, closing connection
```
##### Importance
<!--
Please let us know if the issue is affecting you in a production environment
-->
```
Trying to upgrade 3.4.7
```

It appears that `lib/exabgp/reactor/network/tcp.py` used to wrap the whole MD5 function in `if md5` but that moved to line 129,  and doesn't wrap the `setsockopt()` anymore.

```python
if md5:
    # .. do stuff ..

# does this even if no md5 is configured (line 173)
io.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_TCP, TCP_MD5SIG, sockaddr + key)
```

issue_title: How to distinguish announce and withdraw for BGP Flow Spec from receiver side
issue_body:
Bonjour!

Next step of tests.

I execute following commands from announce peer:
```bash
fl=open("/var/run/exabgp.cmd", "w")
fl.write("announce flow route {\\n match {\\n source 10.0.0.1/32;\\n destination 1.2.3.4/32;\\n }\\n then {\\n discard;\\n }\\n }\\n\n")
fl.flush()
fl.write("withdraw flow route {\\n match {\\n source 10.0.0.1/32;\\n destination 1.2.3.4/32;\\n }\\n then {\\n discard;\\n }\\n }\\n\n")
fl.flush()
````

issue_title: Unable to subsequently update Docker Service when using 'networks'.
issue_body:
Since a few weeks it's no longer possible to perform a call to update() when networks are being defined. For instance:

service.update( name = service.name
                         , image = updated_image
                         , networks = networks
                         #  ... omitted other parameters.
                         )

Previously would refresh the service, only modifying the image. Now it throws the following error:
"rpc error: code = 12 desc = networks must be migrated to TaskSpec before being changed"

Please note, that the first update() works, this only occurs after performing an update() again on a service which was previously updated using the update() function.

For us this is critical, as currently rolling deployments (nightly builds) are no longer being deployed automatically.

issue_title: --format text output is inconsistent
issue_body:
In some commands the text output is an ascii-art table.  (See #168 about changing this to --format table.)  For example:

$ globus bookmark list
Name                                | Endpoint ID                          | Bookmark ID                          | Path                               
----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ | -----------------------------------
midway/~/myproject                  | af7bda53-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec | d89d697e-8cce-11e5-9971-22000b96db58 | /~/myproject/                      

In some commands the text output is a set of Name:Value pairs, each on their own line.  For example:

$ globus bookmark show 13fddf3e-4e77-11e6-8235-22000b97daec
ID:          13fddf3e-4e77-11e6-8235-22000b97daec
Name:        My favorite project
Endpoint ID: af7bda53-6d04-11e5-ba46-22000b92c6ec
Path:        /~/myproject/

I assume the rule is that multi-record output uses the table format, where-as single-record output uses the Name:Value output.  I think we should be more consistent about this.

I suggest we name each of these output format distinctly: "--format table" vs "--format record".  For the record based output, if there are multiple output records, have a blank link between each record.

Note that record-based output should be consistent with aligning the value or not.  "globus bookmark show" aligns the values so that they all start at the same column.  But "globus whoami --verbose" does not, as it just has a single space between the : and the value.

### What was wrong?

Like #600 it would be great to prevent flake8 issues being the only thing preventing a merge. Probably with a shared git hook.

### How can it be fixed?

`flake8` does not have any automatic code cleanup mechanism. Probably have to use something like [yapf](https://github.com/google/yapf#knobs), which requires turning a lot of knobs to mimic our preferred coding style.

issue_title: Allow specifying KDF function for `web3.eth.account.encrypt` API
issue_body:
Migrated from: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/issues/583

### What was wrong?

`eth-keyfile` supports passing in a `kdf` parameter to specify the key derivation function, but this api is not exposed via the `web3.eth.account.encrypt` API.


### How can it be fixed?

Add an optional keyword parameter `kdf` to allow specifying what KDF you want.

Extra options:

Allow setting the default KDF using both environment variables and via a property on the `web3.eth.account` module.

```python
web3.eth.account.defaultKDF = 'scrypt'
```

issue_title: UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
issue_body:
It seems that conda packages, which used to install without problems are now causing this error (on my mac pro):

in:
```
$ conda install -c bioconda secapr
```
out:
```
UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - python 3.6*
  - secapr -> cogent -> python 2.7* -> openssl 1.0.1*
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.

```
I understand that it has something to do with different versions of conda packages being called by different dependencies (I found similar issues in this repo, but the solutions require manual dependency alterations by the users), but in the past (e.g. two weeks ago) this used to work without problems. It would be great to find a fix on the developer side rather than having the user solve package specifications. What can I do to update secapr in order to avoid this issue?
Btw, I got a different error message when trying to install this package on a linux system:

in:
```
$ conda install -c bioconda secapr
```
out:
```
UnsatisfiableError: The following specifications were found to be in conflict:
  - bottleneck
  - phyluce
Use "conda info <package>" to see the dependencies for each package.
```

issue_title: Conda 4.4.x does not prevent removal of conda dependencies from base environment?
issue_body:
Some user tried to remove `pycosat` from within Navigator using conda 4.4.8 
 as reported  on [this issue](https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/8563)

It seems conda 4.4.x is allowing user to remove conda dependencies like `pycosat` which effectively leaves them in a broken state. I checked with 4.4.9 and 4.4.10 and it seems to be that this is still possible.

I have tested on Mac, @jjhelmus tested on Linux. Need to check on windows

issue_title: Suggestion for unsatisfiable packages hint
issue_body:
A lot of issues that are reported com down to, in some sense, the unsatisfiable package specifications hint being not particularly helpful. e.g. #517,  #1801, https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/556, others.

One thing I noticed when stepping through is that (at least in all of the cases that I was looking at), the logging in [prune_packages](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L458) is actually the quite useful. Especially once the minimal subset of the matchspecs has been identified, the error often comes in that method, and the package that gets pruned down to zero matching files is often the "critical" one has a dependency that can't be met.

For example, currently the triple `('python 3.5*', 'numpy 1.9*', 'statsmodels')` is unsatisfiable on win-64, and the "hint" is obviously just that they conflict. This isn't too helpful because you go look and see a `statsmodels-0.6.1-np19py35` package available in the index. But if you look in `prune_packages`, you see pretty that the cause is really `patsy`, which gets pruned to zero doesn't have a np19py35 release. 

I'm sure this won't help in all cases, but as a heuristic, I think that if toposort the matchspecs and then 
applying the pruning, the first thing to crash out might be a pretty good hint to report.

issue_title: conda Python 3.5 install freezes in PowerShell ISE
issue_body:
I have Anaconda with Python 2.7 installed and I was trying to get an additional, parallel installation of Python 3.5.  I used the instructions here:
[Create a Python 3.5 environment](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/py2or3.html)

In Win7, I open a PowerShell ISE with admin privileges, and run:
`$ conda create -n python35 python=3.5 anaconda
`It prints the message "The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED", listed a bunch of packages, and then did nothing for 9 hours. No description of what was executing or stalling or anything.

I tried again several times to no avail.  I tried again with the --debug option, and yet there's still no printouts after the list of packages.

I tried running the same thing in regular PowerShell, and to my surprise it worked.  So, it looks like conda doesn't play well with PowerShell ISE.

For example if you have a yaml with this action:

actions:

    options:

issue_title: Unhelpful error message when git branch does not exist.
issue_body:
I had problems with failed function app deployments which had a sourcecontrols resource.

The branch name was of the form 'feature/do-something-new' which I eventually realised needed to be escaped (i.e. "branch": "[replace(variables('branch'), '/', '\/')]",). I was also able to replicate the deployment error by using a branch name which didn't exist (masterxxx).

My issue is specifically with the error message appearing in the activity log in the portal which was the uninformative:

```
        "statusCode": "Conflict",
        "serviceRequestId": "716e6819-cdfe-4fc5-b34e-d1528f1f4b60",
        "statusMessage": "{\"status\":\"Failed\",\"error\":{\"code\":\"ResourceDeploymentFailure\",\"message\":\"The resource operation completed with terminal provisioning state 'Failed'.\"}}"
```

Interestingly when opening the function app in the portal after the failed deployment with the unescaped 'feature/do-something-new' branch name the source code and functions were visible but not when opening the app following deployment using the unknown branch masterxxx.

The working template can be seen [here](https://github.com/nhsuk/service-etl-function-app/pull/14)

issue_title: During tests: the on_startup signal is sent before the loop is set during
issue_body:
## Long story short

Between the version 2.1 and 2.2 we change the order of when the signal on_startup is sent during the tests and when we make the handlers. Because of that, all the `on_startup` hooks doesn't have a loop in `app.loop`.

https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/commit/ed28add756e2a5dc8caf082641ac1d1810a18c7a

## Expected behaviour

Keep the behavior consistent when running the app within or without a test.

## Actual behaviour

`app.loop` is None during a test but it is an event loop when running with `web.run_app`.

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import aiohttp
from aiohttp import web
from aiohttp.test_utils import AioHTTPTestCase, unittest_run_loop
from distutils.version import StrictVersion


async def hook(app):
    assert StrictVersion(aiohttp.__version__) >= StrictVersion("2.2")
    assert app.loop is not None # fails in the test case but not in run_app


class TestCase(AioHTTPTestCase):
    async def get_application(self):
        app = web.Application()
        app.on_startup.append(hook)
        return app

    @unittest_run_loop
    async def test_on_startup_hook(self):
        pass # will fail before executing the test


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = web.Application()
    app.on_startup.append(hook)
    web.run_app(app)
```

## Your environment

ArchLinux, Python 3.6

issue_title: Client timeouts when raise_for_status is used with connection limit
issue_body:
## Long story short

When using connection limit in TCPConnector and `raise_for_status=True`, when exception is thrown the connection cannot be reused and further requests raise `asyncio.TimeoutError`.

## Expected behaviour

After the exception is raised, the connection is cleaned up and can be reused

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web


async def working_client(loop):
    print("START WORKING")
    timeout = aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=3)  # is not relevant to the test case, just to show error faster
    tcp_connector = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=1)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=tcp_connector, timeout=timeout) as session:
        for _ in range(2):
            try:
                async with session.get("http://localhost:8742/plop") as response:
                    print((await response.read())[:50], '...')
                    response.raise_for_status()
            except aiohttp.ClientResponseError as e:
                print("RETRYING FOR status =", e.status)
    print("END WORKING")


async def not_working_client(loop):
    print("START NOT WORKING")
    timeout = aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=3)  # is not relevant to the test case, just to show error faster
    tcp_connector = aiohttp.TCPConnector(limit=1)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=tcp_connector, timeout=timeout, raise_for_status=True) as session:
        for _ in range(2):
            try:
                async with session.get("http://localhost:8742/plop") as response:
                    print(await response.read())
            except aiohttp.ClientResponseError as e:
                print("RETRYING FOR status =", e.status)
    print("END NOT WORKING")


r404 = False


@asyncio.coroutine
def handler(request):
    global r404
    r404 = not r404
    if r404:
        return aiohttp.web.Response(status=404, body="A" * 200_000)  # this needs to be big, cannot reproduce bug with small body

    return aiohttp.web.Response(body="OK")


@asyncio.coroutine
def main_server(loop):
    global server
    server = aiohttp.web.Server(handler)
    yield from loop.create_server(server, "0.0.0.0", 8742)


# case preparation : craft a server with big writes
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main_server(loop))

# execution of the client
loop.run_until_complete(working_client(loop))
loop.run_until_complete(not_working_client(loop))

# garbage collecting
loop.run_until_complete(server.shutdown(10))
```

```
START WORKING
b'AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA' ...
RETRYING FOR status = 404
b'OK' ...
END WORKING
START NOT WORKING
RETRYING FOR status = 404
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "raise.py", line 61, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(not_working_client(loop))
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 467, in run_until_complete
    return future.result()
  File "raise.py", line 28, in not_working_client
    async with session.get("http://localhost:8742/plop") as response:
  File "/Users/psileikis/projects/tsunami/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 843, in __aenter__
    self._resp = await self._coro
  File "/Users/psileikis/projects/tsunami/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client.py", line 387, in _request
    await resp.start(conn)
  File "/Users/psileikis/projects/tsunami/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/client_reqrep.py", line 761, in start
    self._continue = None
  File "/Users/psileikis/projects/tsunami/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiohttp/helpers.py", line 677, in __exit__
    raise asyncio.TimeoutError from None
concurrent.futures._base.TimeoutError
```
## Your environment

     aiohttp==3.3.2
     Mac OS 10.13.5, but the bug first surfaced on centos-release-7-5.1804.el7.centos.2.x86_64 so it should be widespread
     
     This is related to aiohttp client only.

issue_title: Bug in einsum grad?
issue_body:
Hi,

First, thanks for the great software! I use it regularly.

I've run into a possible bug in the gradient function for numpy's einsum. Here's a reproducible example for my system (pip environment file is attached to this issue).

```{python}
import autograd
import autograd.numpy as np

rng = np.random.RandomState(0)
x = rng.normal(size=(3, 2, 3, 2))

def f(x):
    y = np.einsum('abcd->bd', x)
    return np.sum(y**2)

print(autograd.grad(f)(x))
```

It appears that the indexing operation [here](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/autograd/numpy/numpy_grads.py#L406) triggers the exception. `naked_summed_dims` is a tuple, but I believe it should be a list.

Is this fix as easy as changing `naked_summed_dims` to `list(naked_summed_dims)` in the index? If so, I'm happy to submit a PR.

Thanks for your help!

[env.txt](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/files/1072367/env.txt)

Because of this line, which distributes a minimum of two tests to each node: https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/blob/master/xdist/dsession.py#L369

This came as a surprise to me, because this fact is undocumented, and py.test output doesn't make clear that this is happening. For users running slower tests, such as Selenium functional tests, full parallelization is often desirable. Could the minimum be changed to 1, or made configurable?

issue_title: Suppressing metadata dump in the terminal report
issue_body:
I'm using pytest-metadata in a [plugin to create JSON reports](https://github.com/numirias/pytest-json-report/), similar to how pytest-html uses it, and someone asked for an option to suppress the metadata [dump in the terminal report](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-metadata/blob/1363d9680c583d54d9a87b5d9fa5f48542f66a6c/pytest_metadata/plugin.py#L73).

Would an option for that be possible? Personally, I'd even favor an opt-in (or a configurable length limit) because any plugin which has pytest-metadata as a dependency, and potentially adds new objects with a large repr string to it, will unconditionally write all metadata to the terminal, even if not intended.

issue_title: does new Consumer.subscribe() add to or replace previous subscriptions?

issue_title: Adjust `_tzinfo` implementation to return _DatetimeWithFold or equivalent.
issue_body:
Moving this discussion from #57.

Per the discussion there, I am convinced that @abalkin's PEP-495-consistent implementation of ambiguous time handling [in this mini-project](https://github.com/abalkin/tz) is the right way to do things if we want to be consistent across versions when PEP-495 goes into effect.

The short form is that we'd add a class like this:

``` python
    class _DatetimeWithFold(datetime):
        __slots__ = ()

        @property
        def fold(self):
            return 1
```

And rather than spawning new instances of `_tzinfo` to handle fold behavior, we'd handle fold behavior with `fold = getattr(dt, fold, 0)`.

PEP-495 has some (to me) counter-intuitive choices, so we should probably make an effort to thoroughly document timezone behavior.

issue_title: Valid byte representations of UUIDs fail
issue_body:
If a byte representation of a UUID is passed into a marshmallow UUID field it will be flagged as invalid.  This is because the UUID constructor does not tell the difference between a hex string and a byte representation and will try and parse the byte representation as a hex string and fail.  To create a UUID from a byte representation the bytes must be passed into a `bytes` kwarg in the constructor.

Many databases use the byte representation of UUIDs because it is more compact and indexing is more efficient.  For example this package: https://sqlalchemy-utils.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data_types.html#module-sqlalchemy_utils.types.uuid .  While there is an option to store the UUIDs as chars it is less efficient and other users that are dealing with legacy systems that already have byte representations of UUIDs will not be able to use the UUID field in marshmallow.  Since this is a general issue involving UUIDs an not platform specific I thought it appropriate for the marshmallow core.

The change is small and I already made a pull request #625 .

issue_title: Precision@k evaluation
issue_body:
Looking at the source for the precision at k implementation, it looks like it divides the sum of the true positives by k, which is fine. However, as the wikipedia article states, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evaluation_measures_(information_retrieval)#Precision_at_K ,  one shortcoming of this is that a perfect recommender can still get a low score if the number of test interactions is lower than k. So one implementation I've seen to remedy this is to take min `min(k, num_test_items)` so that it still works in the traditional sense, but also is adaptive for small test sets. What do you think?

Ref implementation: https://gist.github.com/mblondel/7337391#file-letor_metrics-py-L38

issue_title: Nonzero direct irradiance contribution to sky diffuse when sun is behind the array
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
In the `irradiance.py` module the `isotropic`, `klucher`, `haydavies`, `reindl`, and `king` functions have an `Rb` term that contributes an irradiance adjustment to the front-side diffuse even when the sun is behind the array or below the horizon. Under such conditions the only contributions to in-plane sky diffuse should be isotropic sky diffuse and (where modeled) horizon sky diffuse, and should not include the circumsolar component (particularly when this component is computed as a negative value).

**To Reproduce**
Run the code from the `test_total_irrad` function in `test_irradiance.py`. The second `ephem_data` row happens to represent this geometric condition as input... the test does not currently verify the values thus obtained, but if they are examined the behavior can be observed. For example, using the "haydavies" model the sky diffuse result is 20.4696793893283, but it should be 28.439461728635 because the `Rb` term is introducing a negative circumsolar irradiance contribution.

**Expected behavior**
Sky diffuse irradiance that does not vary significantly based on the sun apparent position when that position is behind the array or below the horizon.

I think that this can be fixed by setting `Rb=0` whenever `cos_tt` is negative or `cos_sun_zenith` is negative.

**Versions:**
 - ``pvlib.__version__``: '0.5.2+20.g09126e9'
 - ``pandas.__version__``: '0.23.1'
 - python: '3.6.6 |Anaconda, Inc.| (default, Jun 28 2018, 11:27:44) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)]'

issue_title: Handle common S3 errors human-friendly
issue_body:
There are two common error cases that arise from human mistakes or problems that are out of control of the using team. These errors should have proper log messages instead of stack traces so that the analyzing team can easily grasp what went wrong and where they might have to look:

* **404 Not Found**: if the credentials are not found in the S3 bucket, they where either not synced or not configured. In any case, give a proper message like "ERROR: Credentials not found in S3 bucket."
* **403 Forbidden**: if the server does not have access to the credentials files. In this case, the user has to fix his IAM instance profile. Give a message like "ERROR: Server does not have access to its credentials; fix the IAM profile."

This will catch 95% of the common errors related to berry problems.

There is also at least one argument mismatch that I encountered: --help lists the "--makefiles" flag, but if you specify it, the script fails. Looking through the source reveals why: "--onlymakefiles" is the flag's name and not "--makefiles".

Now after trying "--onlymakefiles" the script still fails, this time because it's unable to find a c++ file, rendering this flag completely useless.


Is there a reason for not using e.g. python's argparse and calling it a day?

Please don't see this as criticism, I just want to understand the reasons behind the build system's design. I'm more than glad to help out with the Linux specific parts, if you need any help -- but for this I need a more precise specification of what you actually want :)

Geoff

I'm trying to run the the below code from the MeiterLab example notebook to create an extension. For some reason, only the namespace.yaml but not the extension.yaml gets written on export so that the load of the namespace fails due to missing file. 

```
from pynwb.spec import NWBNamespaceBuilder, NWBGroupSpec, NWBAttributeSpec, NWBDatasetSpec
from pynwb import get_class, load_namespaces
import os

# Define where our extensions should be stored
output_dir = "'/Volumes/Data Drive/nwb_convert_tutorial"
ext_output_dir  = os.path.join(output_dir, "extensions")

issue_title: Auto conversion of currency is a really bad default, plus you can't change it
issue_body:
I'm talking about the behaviour added here:

https://github.com/django-money/django-money/commit/edfc25a5aa46a605f27d15b891bfae28b74cb5db

which I discovered when upgrading from 0.7.2

Can I persuade you this is a really bad idea? I think it is a very bad idea for any auto-conversion to be built into the money class at all, but having it on by default, and without a way to turn it off is really really bad, because:

1) currency conversion is not a lossless process. You simply can't "compare" two objects in different currencies, nor add them, or subtract them. You can't "compare" potatoes to tomatoes simply because you can use one to buy the other, and for the same reason you can't do the same with currencies, because the price changes all the time. In fact, even at a single point in time there is not one answer - there are many answers, different bodies will trade the commodity at different prices. Exchange rate indexes are just averages.

In addition to changing market exchange rates, there could be any number of additional things you want to do when changing currency. For example, because of rate changes, it's entirely possible that what someone was supposed to pay is no longer the 'right' price. You need to have a strategy for coping with that (e.g. rounding a bit) and silent currency conversions add confusion not help.

Instead, if you want to compare currencies in any way, you should be explicitly doing a conversion. "Explicit is better than implicit" etc. As Python matures, it always gets closer to that ideal e.g. byte strings and unicode cannot be compared in Python 3.

In this way, if you accidentally compare or add two money objects of different currencies, you made a mistake as a developer, and should be informed with a loud exception.

2) You are changing the behaviour of another library.

With plain py-moneyed, as above, you get the desired behaviour:

    >>> Money(10, GBP) > Money(10, USD)
    TypeError: Cannot compare Money with different currencies.

    >>> Money(10, GBP) + Money(10, USD)
    TypeError: Cannot add or subtract two Money instances with different currencies.

It's extremely surprising when django-money doesn't behave the same way, when it is supposed to be built on top of it, and is advertised as such. This exception is extremely important part of how py-moneyed works and what I depend on for ensuring correctness of my app. I happen to use djmoney_rates too, but only ever with explicit conversions.

This is also a really big change from 0.7.2, when it did behave the same as py-moneyed.

3) currency conversion introduces rounding that you didn't ask for, even if the currencies are the same - see https://github.com/evonove/django-money-rates/blob/master/djmoney_rates/utils.py#L55

4) currency conversion kicks off IO! It calls djmoney-rates which calls get_rate_source(), which does DB work, and potentially any kind of DB/network in order to get rates, depending on the backend. So instead of being a pure function, we get network/disk etc., including potential failures because of that.

5) due to implementation of djmoney-rates, this happens even if no conversion is involved - get_rate_source() is called when you are converting to the same currency! This is a massive performance and reliability hit.

A quick check on my machine shows that adding two MoneyPatched instances has gone from 11.1 microseconds in 0.7.2 to 2.8 milliseconds i.e. 250 times slower, including 5 DB queries for *EVERY* money comparison/sum, including ones that require NO conversion because they are in the same currency! And this is in the best case when the rates have already been fetched over the network, and the database has warmed up and is in the OS cache etc.

At the very least, the feature should be selected with a setting, which should default to False. I would argue the feature should be removed entirely - if you are dealing with money, you will *always* want to be explicit about conversions. There may be a few extremely simple use cases where the strategy of auto conversion is good enough, but a generic money app should not be doing that.

issue_title: page.url property caching?
issue_body:
Hi all,

This is another new issue that didn't exist in 0.6, but when accessing a `Page` objects `url` property, translation seems to fail.

For example, in a shell:
```
from django.utils import translation
translation.activate('en')
for p in Page.objects.all():
    p.url
    p.slug

translation.activate('fr')
for p in Page.objects.all():
    p.url
    p.slug
```

In the above example, the `url` property will print just fine on the first run, but once the language changes `url` property is blank - although the `slug` property returns the correct value based on the activated language.

This wasn't an issue in 0.6.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: some bugs related to sscanf sim_procedure
issue_body:
Hi,
Firstly, thanks for your great jobs about angr. 
When I use angr to analyse some binaries involving `sscanf ` syscall, there seem to be some bugs.

Take the code below as example:

    #include <stdio.h>      
    void main()
    {
	    char buf[10];
	    char *value = "123456abc";
	    char *fmt = "%*d%s";
	    sscanf(value,fmt,buf);
	    printf("%s\n", buf);	
    }
the corresponding angr code is as follows:

    In [1]: import angr
    
    In [2]: project = angr.Project('./test_sscanf',load_options={'auto_load_libs':False})

    In [3]: pg = project.factory.simgr()
    
    In [4]: pg.run()
    WARNING | 2017-11-01 15:14:13,950 | angr.manager | No completion state defined for 
    SimulationManager; stepping until all states deadend
    Out[4]: <SimulationManager with 1 deadended>

    In [5]: pg.deadended[0].posix.dumps(1)
    Out[5]: ''

There are two issues:

1.  some info should be outputted, but the `posix.dumps(1)` is ''. (Or I misunderstand it? )
2.  the `_parse()` in `FormatParser` can't get the right format_str(`%*d%s`), instead  it gets `%d%s`. 

For the issue 1, I set a breakpoint in `sscanf.py`, and check the content in buf. It does have some
values, although it's incorrect. But why the `posix.dumps(1)` is ''?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13868458/32264916-dbd81428-bf1b-11e7-9415-7258ff88774e.png)
    
For the issue 2,  I roughly have a look at the code in `format_parser.py` , the specifiers are suitable for `printf`/`sprintf` syscall,  but may not be enough to `sscanf` syscall. In my opinion, `sscanf` syscall behaves like regex, so it's more complex.

Note: the angr version I installed is the latest, system is `Ubuntu 16.04 x64`.

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Pathological runtimes with specific CSV files
issue_body:
As reported by @jbednar 

The file `data.csv` has 3 named columns with trailing commas and a trailing space.  `pandas.read_csv()` can handle this fine, while `odo` completes, but takes several hundred times longer to complete.

```
$ head data.csv
a,b,c, 
-1.3700737E7, 6275190.0, w, 
-1.3700711E7, 6275195.0, w, 
-1.3702081E7, 6274898.5, w, 
-1.3701948E7, 6274931.0, w, 
-1.3701793E7, 6275088.5, w, 
-1.3701224E7, 6274994.5, w, 
-1.370176E7, 6274780.0, w, 
-1.370215E7, 6274539.5, w, 
-1.3702123E7, 6274279.0, w, 

$ wc -l data.csv
  306675005 data.csv
```

`odo`ing the first 10**6 lines of this file into a `DataFrame` takes approximately 30s, meaning the entire 300M line file will take upwards of 2.5 hours to load.

```python
In [4]: %time odo('data_1E6.csv', DataFrame)
CPU times: user 29.7 s, sys: 74.6 ms, total: 29.8 s
Wall time: 29.8 s
Out[4]:
        a            b             c Unnamed: 3
0        -13700737    6275190.0    w 2015-10-23
1        -13700711    6275195.0    w 2015-10-23
2        -13702081    6274898.5    w 2015-10-23
3        -13701948    6274931.0    w 2015-10-23
4        -13701793    6275088.5    w 2015-10-23
5        -13701224    6274994.5    w 2015-10-23
6        -13701760    6274780.0    w 2015-10-23
7        -13702150    6274539.5    w 2015-10-23
8        -13702123    6274279.0    w 2015-10-23
9        -13702072    6274537.0    w 2015-10-23
10       -13701917    6274562.5    w 2015-10-23
```

The equivalent `pandas.read_csv()` call takes approximately 3 minutes to load *the entire file*.

The `odo` call has trouble with the trailing commas and trailing spaces.  It extracts an unnamed column, and the key performance killer is that odo [*converts the space in that column into a datetime*](https://github.com/blaze/odo/blob/master/odo/backends/pandas.py#L60-L61).

Fix is forthcoming.

issue_title: Commercial support
issue_body:
I love lib - thanks so much for your hard work, @syrusakbary!

With that said, I'd happily contribute to paying for more of your time to extend the lib, and fill in a few missing pieces that would make it more viable to use in production.

Namely:

- Error catching, per https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/issues/177, and https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/issues/202. System exceptions bubble up to user output by default, which a dangerous default IMO. I'd love to see a cleaner way of logging exceptions and controlling error output that could be defined in one place, rather than defensive `try/except` blocks in every mutation.

- Core improvements / re-factoring. Thread safety (#43), resolving promise issues (https://github.com/syrusakbary/promise/issues/57), moving to `async/await` per [your comment](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/612#issuecomment-347066815), etc. There are a few core things that are beyond my immediate experience with how things work under the hood, that seem like they have some potential to throw weird/unexpected issues that are hard to diagnose.

- Documented subscription support/patterns, per #781.

- Docs synced with releases. There have been a few occasions where trying doc examples has thrown errors, such as #812 today or https://github.com/graphql-python/flask-graphql/issues/52 which I ran into a few days ago. When this happens, monkey-patched workarounds are usually suggested by the community, which makes for brittler code.

- Some more tooling to provide official solutions for #772, etc. Calculating the cost of queries, parsing the AST tree and preempting query joins that might be necessary, etc. Outside the purview of the core, perhaps, but necessary stuff at scale that would save devs reinventing the wheel.

- Closing issues faster. This is just a general thing, but there are issues going back 2-3 years that would be good to get unstuck. Much of it might even be redundant now or have other solutions, but clearing the backlog would make a clearer case for using the lib in production, knowing those same issues are unlikely to resurface.

Obviously, your time is valuable and the fact that you've put together _anything_ at all - let alone something as cool as Graphene - is amazing, so thank you.

But perhaps there's a way, as a community, we could buy more of your time to address some of the above? I'd happily chunk in a few bucks on the reg to keep this lib up-to-date.

issue_title: nxos replace operation - add to docs
issue_body:
Can't use `show run` for initial config of replace operation; need to use a checkpoint file.

```
checkpoint test-checkpoint-kb description some text 
# need all to get defaults
show checkpoint test-checkpoint-kb all
```

need to be careful about copy-and-paste from box as line-wrap can introduce invalid newlines for very long lines.

issue_title: EOSDriver._load_config() doesn't support HEREDOC syntax
issue_body:
If attempting to blindly copy a multiline command from a potential config to a file, conversion is required.
```
with EOSDriver(s, u, p) as d:
    load_merge_candidate(config="banner login\nTHIS IS MY BANNER\nEOF")
...
MergeConfigException: Error [1002]: CLI command 4 of 5 'THIS IS MY BANNER' failed: invalid command [Invalid input (at token 0: 'THIS')]
```

issue_title: Unable to load banner MOTD on Arista
issue_body:
Firstly, I am not entirely sure what's the right format expected. I tried loading:

```text
banner motd
!!aaaaa
EOF
```

With this, it fails:

```python
>>> config = '''banner motd
... !!aaaa
... EOF
... '''
>>> e.load_merge_candidate(config=config)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/napalm/eos/eos.py", line 245, in load_merge_candidate
    self._load_config(filename, config, False)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/napalm/eos/eos.py", line 225, in _load_config
    commands = self._multiline_convert(commands, start=start, depth=depth)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/napalm/eos/eos.py", line 157, in _multiline_convert
    ret[s] = {'cmd': ret[s], 'input': "\n".join(ret[s+1:e])}
TypeError: sequence item 0: expected string or Unicode, dict found
```

Testing against a vEOS running:

```
Software image version: 4.18.1F
Architecture:           i386
Internal build version: 4.18.1F-4591672.4181F
Internal build ID:      6fcb426e-70a9-48b8-8958-54bb72ee28ed
```

Changing the line 157 to:

```diff
diff --git a/napalm/eos/eos.py b/napalm/eos/eos.py
index 4216ae5..bcb07e1 100644
--- a/napalm/eos/eos.py
+++ b/napalm/eos/eos.py
@@ -154,7 +154,7 @@ class EOSDriver(NetworkDriver):
                 depth = depth - 1
         except ValueError:  # Couldn't find end, abort
             return ret
-        ret[s] = {'cmd': ret[s], 'input': "\n".join(ret[s+1:e])}
+        ret[s] = {'cmd': ret[s], 'input': "\n".join([ele['input'] for ele in ret[s+1:e]])}
         del ret[s + 1:e + 1]

         return ret
```

As it looks like `ret` is a list looking like: `[u'configure session napalm_61252', 'banner motd', {u'input': u'aaaa', u'cmd': u'comment'}, 'EOF']`, and the object referenced by `ret[s+1:e]` is a list of dictionaries (not a list of strings).

@bewing I recall you've been working on this, would you recall any details and wondering if on other EOS versions pyeapi returns a list of strings as the code would suggest? I am running pyeapi `0.8.1`.

issue_title: Pyexcel with pyinstaller
issue_body:
Hi, I am trying to get pyexcel work with pyinstaller, and I continually get error:

`Please check if there were typos in function parameters: {'file_name': 'credential.xls'}. Otherwise unrecognized parameters were given.
`

I have followed the website http://pyexcel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pyinstaller.html to include the hidden import for both pyexcel-io and pyexcel-xls but with no luck.

Any help is appreciated, thanks!

Minimal working example:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

issue_title: TFlearn regression maybe implemented incorrectly - looks wrong
issue_body: IMHO that this also relates to #343, #378 and a few others. I am working on converting a stacked LSTM tensor flow example to use tflearn: https://github.com/tgjeon/TensorFlow-Tutorials-for-Time-Series/blob/master/lstm-for-epf.py. It doesn't work with the latest Tensorflow, some of the contrib functions/libraries are no longer available and such. I tried implement the corresponding network in tflearn and digging deeper into the network I found a bug with TFlearn regression implementation. It's not actually implemented. @aymericdamien implemented a tensorflow example of regression https://github.com/aymericdamien/TensorFlow-Examples/blob/master/examples/2_BasicModels/linear_regression.py: `# Construct a linear model` `pred = tf.add(tf.mul(X, W), b)` `# Mean squared error` `cost = tf.reduce_sum(tf.pow(pred-Y, 2))/(2*n_samples)` `# Gradient descent` `optimizer = tf.train.GradientDescentOptimizer(learning_rate).minimize(cost) ` Well looking at the simple regression example with tflearn at https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/basics/linear_regression.py you get a completely different graph - devoid of weights, biases and formula. You get just y. Please find the pictures below - one with regression that works with tensorflow and the other with tflearn. I suspect @aymericdamien could fix this in a jiffy by putting in weights and biases and the normal formula for linear regression. Just to help out, there is a function in `from tensorflow.python.ops import nn`, that has `nn.xw_plus_b(tensor_in, weights, biases)`. The non-working version: The working version: Thanks in advance, Andrew

issue_title: What is the difference between textVector and sentenceVector?
issue_body: In the source code, textVector() and sentenceVector() are both for generating the vector of a series of words. If the model is supervised text classification model, textVector() is used, and sentenceVector in other case. The only difference between them is that sentence vector is normed but textVector not. What is the reason for this?

issue_title: Investigate refactoring to allow incorporation of prompts from python-prompt-toolkit
issue_body:
Python Prompt Toolkit provides some really powerful and cool features:
https://github.com/jonathanslenders/python-prompt-toolkit

It would be nice if a cmd2 user could optionally replace the default prompt with a prompt from python-prompt-toolkit.

This would at a minimum require replacing the simple string prompt with a function which prints a prompt and returns the text the user entered.  It would be somewhat of a departure from cmd.  But it would open up the door for easily incorporating things like syntax-highlighting, input validation, and a bottom toolbar.

I'm not sure it would really be possible to make cmd2 and python-prompt-toolkit play nicely together, but if it is, it would be a great thing.

issue_title: Potentially misleading "File not found" error when verify value is quoted
issue_body:
I'm using vdirsyncer 0.14.1 with Python 3.5.1 on openSUSE Tumbleweed.  I'm connecting to a Radicale CardDAV server with a self-signed SSL certificate.  This version of vdirsyncer warns the user to quote all strings in the configuration file.  I therefore (mistakenly, as it turns out) quoted the value of the "verify" property in my remote storage section:
```
verify = "false"
```

    error: Unknown error occured: [Errno 2] No such file or directory
    error: Use `-vdebug` to see the full traceback.
    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/cli/utils.py", line 518, in _worker

    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/cli/tasks.py", line 88, in discover_collections

    debug:     list_collections=list_collections))
    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/cli/utils.py", line 333, in _collections_for_pair_impl
    debug:     b_discovered = _discover_from_config(pair.config_b)
    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/cli/utils.py", line 268, in _discover_from_config
    debug:     return handle_storage_init_error(cls, config)
    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/cli/utils.py", line 264, in _discover_from_config

    debug:   File "/home/psy/opt/vdirsyncer/lib/python3.5/site-packages/vdirsyncer/utils/http.py", line 58, in request

issue_title: Have install not require `--cert-path`, `--fullchain-path` or `--key-path` parameters
issue_body:
Feature request as this won't work without `--cert-path`, `--fullchain-path` or `--key-path` parameters:

```
./certbot-auto install --noninteractive --apache --domains pcm.4delphi.com
```

But this will request and install without any extra parameters:

```
./certbot-auto --noninteractive --apache --domains pcm.4delphi.com
```

And this will request without any extra parameters:

```
./certbot-auto certonly --noninteractive --apache --domains pcm.4delphi.com
```

So please make `install` as smart as `certonly` or no command at all that somehow figure out the correct locations of the certificates.

--jeroen

issue_title: UserMixin.expiration_timedelta() returns wrong value because timedelta calculates for different timezones
issue_body:
I am using google oauth2 with django. The UserMixin provides expiration check by access_token_expired() that executes expiration_timedelta() inside.
So, the problem is in expiration_timedelta. Code of the method:
```python
        if self.extra_data and 'expires' in self.extra_data:
            try:
                expires = int(self.extra_data.get('expires'))
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                return None

            now = datetime.utcnow()

            # Detect if expires is a timestamp
            if expires > time.mktime(now.timetuple()):
                # expires is a datetime, return the remaining difference
                return datetime.fromtimestamp(expires) - now
            else:
                # expires is the time to live seconds since creation,
                # check against auth_time if present, otherwise return
                # the value
                auth_time = self.extra_data.get('auth_time')
                if auth_time:
                    reference = datetime.fromtimestamp(auth_time)
                    return (reference + timedelta(seconds=expires)) - now
                else:
                    return timedelta(seconds=expires)

```

The problem occurs because we use UTC now time for current moment, but we use LOCAL time when executing `datetime.fromtimestamp`.
How it works?
Imagine that I am in +2 timezone and right now I have a 12 o'clock UTC and expiration time is 3600 seconds. I got access_token and refresh_token at 11 o'clock UTC, so, my token is already expired. But, when I get auth_time then I'll get 13 o'clock LOCAL + expiration time = 14 o'clock. Timedelta is 14 o'clock LOCAL subtract 12 o'clock UTC = 2 hours(It's strange, but python calculates it like these datetimes at the same timezone). And then we check expiration_timedelta().total_seconds() <= 0 and we got false instead of true.

The fix is very simple: use `datetime.utcfromtimestamp` instead of `datetime.fromtimestamp`.
I do that expiration check manually in my project right now and all works smoothly.

I'll provide a pull request with fix.

issue_title: Autoscaler should enable map_public_ip_on_launch for subnet it uses
issue_body:
I had to set `MapPublicIpOnLaunch` attribute to get autoscaler to get past the problem of autoscaler creating instances without public ips. I think more generally autoscaler should create its own subnet with proper setting than reuse a random existing subnet. An example of creating subnets is in https://github.com/diux-dev/cluster/blob/master/create_resources.py

```
ec2_resource1=boto3.resource('ec2', 'us-east-1')
bad_subnet=ec2_resource1.Subnet('subnet-79920c56')
client1 = boto3.client('ec2', 'us-east-1')
bad_subnet.map_public_ip_on_launch #=> False

response = client1.modify_subnet_attribute(
    MapPublicIpOnLaunch={
        'Value': True
    },
    SubnetId='subnet-79920c56'
)
bad_subnet.map_public_ip_on_launch #=> True

```

from tqdm import tqdm
from . import limits

def like(self, media_id):
    if limits.check_if_bot_can_like(self):

        if super(self.__class__, self).like(media_id):
            self.total_liked += 1
            return True
    else:

    for media in tqdm(medias):

issue_title: Make Django middleware compatible with both version below and above 1.10
issue_body: Django 1.10 added a new middleware interface in `settings.py` called `MIDDLWARE` instead of `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES`. Current implementation only supports the `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` interface. We need to make the middleware compatible with both interfaces. Django provides [MiddlewareMixin](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/topics/http/middleware/#upgrading-pre-django-1-10-style-middleware) to solve this problem.

issue_title: Encode array of string arrays
issue_body: The VLenArray codec currently supports encoding of an array of arrays of scalars. It would be useful if an array of arrays of (variable length) strings could also be supported. E.g., support ``VLenArray('str')`` and use VLenUTF8 internally to encode each sub-array.

issue_title: How to write the regex for escape string
issue_body:
### Lark Grammar
regex_lit             : /\/[^\/]*\//

### Question
the string is
```python
a = '/^Connect2Id GET \/customClaimsMap\.properties Successes$/ AND /^test$/'
```
a `re pattern with a escape string`
when i use lark , i got 
```
`/^Connect2Id GET \\/`
```
 but i expect is 
```
`/^Connect2Id GET \/customClaimsMap\.properties Successes$/`
```
so, what's the right re pattern? can help?
